We examine two career pathways to the true self at work: moral careers (e.g., those in healthcare, social work), which should foster authenticity because morality is central to identity and the true self, and aesthetic careers (e.g., those in artistic and creative fields), which should foster authenticity through the freedom and autonomy they afford. Across three experiments (N = 1,232) and one archival study using the General Social Survey (GSS; N = 72,390), we compare these pathways from both third- and first-person perspectives. Study 1 shows that job seekers value the freedom and autonomy we theorize aesthetic careers provide. Study 2 shows that third-party observers expect aesthetic careers to afford greater freedom, access to the true self, happiness, and life satisfaction than moral careers. Study 3 uses the GSS and reveals the opposite pattern: people in moral careers report greater happiness and job satisfaction than those in aesthetic or control careers. Study 4 reconciles this discrepancy by directly comparing third- and first-person judgments about the true self. We find that expectations about aesthetic careers largely match reality, but that observers systematically underestimate the psychological benefits of moral careers. Together, these findings suggest that aesthetic careers offer a path to the true self and happiness at work, while the comparable, and sometimes greater, benefits of moral careers go underappreciated.

articles.title: Hard bargains and even splits: Fairness judgments track bargaining power across diverse cultures
articles.authors: Arthur Le Pargneux; Xavier Roberts-Gaal; Lucas Woodley; Joshua D. Greene; Fiery Andrews Cushman
articles.abstract:
What is the fair way to distribute resources? Past research highlights egalitarian and redistributive intuitions that favor the disadvantaged. Contractualist theories propose moral judgments mirror what rational agents would agree to, favoring parties in advantaged bargaining positions. We reconcile these views, showing people think it fair to favor the advantaged when they have more bargaining power, but to favor the disadvantaged in a non-bargaining context. Specifically, across nine countries, participants (n = 6,304) make third-party judgments about the
morally best split of a fixed amount. When the split requires agreement (bilateral negotiation in Study 1; third-party negotiation in Study 2), moral judgments overwhelmingly track bargaining power and can be predicted with striking quantitative precision. When the split is determined by
a unilateral donation and existing inequalities do not alter bargaining positions, moral intuitions
are reversed, reflecting redistributive or egalitarian concerns.

articles.title: TuneTwins: An Intrinsically Motivating Game Paradigm for Scalable Music Cognition Research
articles.authors: Jiaxin Li; David John Baker; John Ashley Burgoyne; Henkjan Honing
articles.abstract: Researchers across the behavioral sciences are increasingly using games as a means of data collection. Intrinsically motivating digital games are especially promising for music cognition, where large, diverse, and engaged samples are needed. In this article, we focus on a specific class of game paradigms suited to cross-cultural contexts and applicable across the developmental lifespan: auditory matching-pairs (Memory) games. We present the conception, design, and initial evaluation of TuneTwins, a solitaire auditory matching-pairs paradigm developed to study musical memory and auditory cognition. Building on self-determination theory and prior game-based research, TuneTwins employs an 8-pair (4 × 4) board to balance playability with psychometric sensitivity. Our web-based auditory adaptation introduces clear visual–auditory mappings, a three-click turn structure, and a dual scoring system that distinguishes memorized matches from lucky guesses and memory errors. Large-scale simulations of chance and optimal strategies guided the choice of board size, demonstrating that this configuration yields meaningful separation between performance extremes while avoiding ceiling effects. We further report empirical evidence from three implementations: a proof-of-concept online study, a cross-cultural field study with Mbendjele BaYaka hunter-gatherers, and an online global deployment. Across these settings, TuneTwins captured systematic performance differences between stimulus conditions and elicited high voluntary engagement, including repeated play and the completion of multiple difficulty levels. We argue that matching-pairs games such as TuneTwins provide an engaging, intuitive, and scalable paradigm for collecting behavioral data using primarily auditory stimuli—and that they can be used to probe the mental representation of music across cultures, across development, and potentially across species.

articles.title: The effects of introducing measurement-based care in outpatient psychotherapy: a naturalistic study
articles.authors: Tim Kaiser; Selin Demir; Eva-Lotta Brakemeier
articles.abstract:
Objective. This study investigated the effectiveness of a newly implemented routine outcome monitoring and feedback system, the Greifswald Psychotherapy Navigation System (GPNS). 
Methods. 261 patients (M_age = 33 years, 79% female) completed individual cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) from 61 psychotherapists. Using Bayesian instrumental variable estimation, we estimated the local average treatment effect (LATE) of using the GPNS on post-treatment symptom severity and personality impairment. Sensitivity analyses were performed to estimate the robustness of our estimates.
Results. Using the GPNS significantly reduced post-treatment symptom severity (LATE = -0.11, 95% CrI: -0.18; -0.03) and personality impairment (LATE = -2.65, 95% CrI: -3.72; -1.58). Sensitivity analyses showed that the effects were robust to plausible residual confounding. 
Conclusion. The GPNS shows promise for improving outcomes of CBT. Future prospective trials should be conducted to obtain a more generalizable estimate of its effect.

articles.title: Economic Growth and Emotional Well-Being: Longitudinal Evidence from 33 European Countries
articles.authors: Filip Fors Connolly; Mikael Hjerm; Tommy Gärling
articles.abstract: The relationship between economic growth and emotional well-being remains poorly understood relative to life satisfaction, largely due to methodological limitations. Using psychometrically robust measures we examined how national income relates to emotional well-being. Analyzing 178,948 participants across 33 European countries in four European Social Survey waves (2006, 2012, 2014, 2023), we constructed a reliable emotional well-being measure and compared it with single-item life satisfaction. Both showed strong positive cross-sectional correlations with GDP per capita. Linear mixed-effects models revealed economic growth significantly predicts within-country increases in both emotional well-being and life satisfaction. These findings demonstrate that economic development improves both cognitive and affective dimensions of subjective well-being, contradicting previous research suggesting a weak association with emotional experiences. Economic growth thus remains important for improving citizens' daily emotional experiences, not merely their life evaluations, highlighting the continued relevance of economic development for well-being.

articles.title: Factors Associated with Students’ Perceived Competence Using Statistical Software
articles.authors: Alyssa Counsell
articles.abstract: Statistical software is used in many statistics courses across a wide range of disciplines. However, minimal research examines how students’ attitudes and experience relate to feeling competent using statistical software by the end of a statistics course. In this study, I examined variables relevant from two competing theories (expectancy-value theory; EVT, and technology acceptance model; TAM) to examine which ones were most strongly associated with students’ perceived competence using statistical software by the end of a statistics course. Participants came from two samples; sample 1 included undergraduate students learning a point-click software in the social sciences (n =110); sample 2 included students learning R in a Masters program in analytics (n = 193). In both samples, the TAM-aligned variables had higher correlations with perceived competence than the EVT-aligned statistics attitudes variables. However, the correlations of EVT-aligned variables tended to be stronger in sample 2. Analysis of open-ended data asking about factors impacting software competence resulted in somewhat different findings across the samples. Sample 1 participants were more likely to focus on course-specific resources such as course manuals or instructor impacts, whereas sample 2 participants were more likely to focus on the importance of real-world applications, previous experience, and intrinsic factors.

articles.title: Delusions are Related to Aberrant Causal Generalization
articles.authors: Martin Meyer; Santiago Castiello; Samuel Paskewitz; Philip R. Corlett
articles.abstract: Generalization tendencies might simultaneously explain the emergence and maintenance of delusional beliefs. On the one hand, people with a tendency to under-generalize information might produce “overfit” explanatory models and accordingly fail to recognize when new data bears on them; on the other hand, people with a tendency to over-generalize information might produce “underfit” explanatory models and accordingly update them in inappropriate scenarios. To adjudicate between these hypotheses, we test whether delusions predict performance on a classic stimulus generalization paradigm (the peak shift). The behavioral data indicate that higher delusionality predicts over-generalization of learned causal relationships and under-generalization of learned absence of causality. Computational modeling largely reveals the driving mechanism to be stochasticity (and perhaps prediction error), however, specific aspects of delusionality (distress, contents) relate to different generalization tendencies.

articles.title: What is the Feasibility and Benefit of Psychosocial Interventions for Adults with Intellectual Disabilities and Associated Mental Health Conditions? A systematic review.
articles.authors: Louise Gates
articles.abstract:
Background: Mental illness is highly prevalent amongst people with ID, and those affected are treated with often complex and sometimes numerous pharmacological treatments. It is clear that high-quality studies are needed to assess the feasibility of implementation of psychosocial interventions as alternative or adjunct treatments.
Methods: One reviewer independently applied the inclusion criteria to the search results and extracted data from the selected studies for inclusion in the review. All papers for inclusion were reviewed by a second reviewer, as were the reasons for excluding other papers. The databases searched were Cochrane, SCOPUS, Medline, AMED, CINHAL, SportsDiscus, PsychARTICLES, and PsychINFO.
Results: Four studies were included in this review. These were the only studies deemed eligible for inclusion and all were rated as having a low risk of bias.
Conclusion: There is a dearth of literature on psychosocial interventions for adults with ID and associated mental health conditions. This review provides evidence that more studies should be undertaken to assess the feasibility and implementation of psychosocial interventions with this vulnerable population group.

articles.title: What is the Feasibility and Scientific Benefit of Laughter Yoga Vs Vinyasa Yoga as a Psychosocial Intervention for Adults Experiencing Depression and Anxiety Secondary to Learning Disability?
articles.authors: Louise Gates; Martin Bollard; Derek Renshaw
articles.abstract:
Background: This feasibility study explored the potential of laughter yoga (LY) compared with vinyasa yoga (VY), as a psychosocial intervention for adults with learning disabilities (LD) experiencing depression and anxiety. No studies have previously investigated the effects of yoga on anxiety and depression in adults with intellectual disabilities, making this the first to assess two yoga styles in this context.
Methods: Thirteen participants were randomly assigned to LY (n=4), vinyasa yoga (VY; n=4), or a control group (n=5). Quantitative measures included mood rating scales, blood pressure, and salivary cortisol, assessed at baseline, week 3, week 6, and week 10. Qualitative data were collected through interviews. LY was found to be feasible and well-received. Results: While no significant effects were observed for depression, blood pressure, or cortisol, anxiety levels showed a significant reduction over time (F (3, 24) = 4.369; p = .014). Thematic analysis revealed five key themes, with two focusing on positive participant feedback and emotional well-being. 
Conclusion: The study demonstrates the acceptability of LY and suggests its potential as a non-pharmacological intervention for mental health in adults with LD. These findings support the need for a full-scale randomized controlled trial to further evaluate LY’s efficacy. This research contributes to the limited evidence base on yoga interventions for adults with LD (Chong et al., 2011; Kataria, 2002; Rao et al., 2013) and highlights the importance of inclusive, adaptable approaches to mental health support.
Keywords: yoga, laughter yoga, vinyasa yoga, anxiety, depression, learning disability, adults

articles.title: Stories for change: A theory informed, co produced method for developing lived experience narratives for behaviour change interventions
articles.authors: Emma Davies; Jennifer Seddon; Abi Rose; Cat Butler; Anne Greaves; Eila Watson; Fiona Matley
articles.abstract:
Objectives: Storytelling is increasingly used in health behaviour change interventions, but there is limited guidance on how to develop stories that are not only engaging and acceptable, but also theoretically robust and capable of functioning as active intervention components. This paper aimed to describe and reflect on a theory-informed, co-produced method for developing lived-experience narratives for behaviour change interventions.
Design: Qualitative interviews, co-production, and acceptability testing within the development of the SPACES intervention for alcohol reduction in mid-life women.
Methods: Lived-experience narratives were generated through 21 biographic-narrative interviews with mid-life women who were considering reducing their drinking or had recently done so. Transcripts were analysed using a structured story-component extraction proforma capturing Character, Context, Motives, Helpers, and Resolution. Extracted material was mapped to COM-B domains, behaviour change techniques, and mechanisms of action, aligned with the intervention logic model, and developed into intervention-ready stories. Stories were further refined through co-production with a public advisory group and acceptability testing with 28 target users.
Results: The method provided a structured way to develop authentic stories while embedding behaviour change content. Preliminary acceptability work suggested that stories were experienced as relatable, supportive, and non-judgemental, and that co-production and feedback were central to refining realism, clarity, and relevance.
Conclusions: Hypothesised mechanisms and efficacy of SPACES remain to be tested. This paper provides practical methodological guidance for developing theory-informed, co-produced stories as components of behaviour change interventions.

articles.title: The risks of conflating anhedonia with apathy
articles.authors: Sijia Zhao; Mercè Correa; John Salamone; Masud Husain
articles.abstract: The capacity for pleasure is distinct from the desire for reward, or the effort required to obtain it. Yet a persistent trend in translational research conflates these dissociable processes, by absorbing motivational dysfunctions such as anergia/fatigue, avolition, and clinical apathy—involving a significant reduction in goal-directed behaviour—into the definition of anhedonia. This is not simply a semantic issue. By collapsing separable aspects of motivation into a single hedonic label, the field risks misdirecting therapeutic development; treatments targeting pleasure may be ineffective for patients whose primary deficit is in behavioural activation. In this Perspective, we propose deconstructing the reward-pursuit cycle into three mechanistically distinct stages — liking, wanting, and acting to fulfil goals—and show that much of what is labelled ‘anhedonia’ in translational models instead reflects impaired behavioural activation, a core feature of clinical apathy. Restoring this distinction is essential for precision phenotyping and mechanism-based treatment development.

articles.title: Cuing the future to reduce delay discounting: EEG correlates of value and vividness
articles.authors: Isaac Kinley; Jana Lau; Donna Rose Addis
articles.abstract: The tendency to discount future reward can be reduced when individuals are cued to imagine future events. This "cue effect" has been studied using fMRI, but the current work is the first to investigate it using EEG. We found that more subjectively vivid imagination of the future encouraged more farsighted decisions, and that subjective vividness was higher when larger delayed rewards were available. Using time-frequency partial least squares analysis, we identified a pattern of enhanced gamma and reduced theta power that correlated with the subjective value of delayed rewards on trials without cues. We also found a pattern of reduced parietal alpha power that was associated with subjective vividness on trials with cues. We contextualize these findings within the broader EEG literature on reward valuation, mental event simulation, and mental imagery.

articles.title: Non-conscious knowledge of complex regularities supports instrumental conditioning: A registered report
articles.authors: Răzvan Jurchiș; Andrei Preda; Andrei Razvan Costea; Lina Skora
articles.abstract: Instrumental conditioning (IC) consists in actively learning to approach rewarding stimuli and to avoid punishing stimuli. This central process for adaptation requires a relatively high computational capacity because it entails the integration of knowledge regarding stimuli, behaviours, and outcomes. Recent studies using subliminal exposure show that IC occurs only for consciously perceived stimuli, suggesting that IC may require awareness. In the present study, we investigated whether IC could occur when implicit processing is stimulated not by subliminal exposure but by employing predictive regularities that are complex and difficult to detect consciously. In a novel IC task based on the artificial grammar learning task, participants gave approach (Go) or avoidance responses (No-Go) to strings from two grammars. Unbeknown to them, one of the grammars was reward-predictive and the other was punishment-predictive. Trial-by-trial measures assessed participants’ awareness of the grammar structures and of their judgments regarding the predictive value of the strings. In both an IC phase and a delayed responding phase, we found that participants responded adaptively when they were aware of the regularities and, also, when they were not. Importantly, we found reliable evidence that non-conscious regularities produced adaptive responding only when they produced conscious judgments regarding the predictive values of the stimuli. When judgment knowledge was unconscious, data were insensitive or more consistent with chance performance. This suggests that IC may be produced by a combination of unconscious learning and conscious integration.

articles.title: Speech activity confounds respiratory sinus arrhythmia reactivity in psychophysiological research: how to detect and control for the effects of speech in daily life
articles.authors: Melisa Saygin; Martin Gevonden; Eco de Geus
articles.abstract: Speaking is a recurring activity that interferes with the measurement of the physiological stress response. Unique respiratory demands of speech amplify respiratory sinus arrhythmia (RSA) when cardiac vagal activity may be unchanged or withdrawn. While this confounding may be experimentally controlled in laboratory settings, a speech activity detection model or, alternatively, an RSA metric that controls for the respiratory demands of speech is needed in daily life research. Here, we first validate an accelerometer-based speech detection model and impedance pneumography-based respiration assessment in naturalistic settings, using a throat microphone and respiratory inductance pneumography (RIP) as respective ground truths. Across participants, the accelerometer-based speech detection model showed good concurrent and criterion validity (71.6% sensitivity, 83.9% specificity, 84.4% AUC, .43 Matthews correlation coefficient). Impedance-based respiratory parameters showed good-to-moderate concurrent and criterion validity for breathing period (.59 ≤ r ≤ .71) and tidal volume (.50 ≤ r ≤ .54) but weak validity for inspiration-to-expiration duration (IE)-ratio (.27 ≤ r ≤ .43). Next, using mixed models, we confirmed that speech significantly shortens interbeat interval (by 75.2 ms) while it, counterintuitively, inflates RSA (by 25.3 ms) within individuals. After normalization by tidal volume, RSA showed the expected decrease in response to speech in sitting postures, but it required further control for inspiration-to-expiration duration (IE)-ratio in standing posture. Examination of individual participant slopes revealed IE-ratio controlled tidal volume-normalized RSA successfully eliminated the speech-related inflation of RSA for all participants. We recommend reporting IE-controlled tidal volume-normalized RSA in protocols involving speech, whether based on laboratory or ambulatory data. As speech also impacts a host of autonomic nervous system-related parameters other than RSA, the validated speech activity detection model can have wider utility to help disentangle psychophysiological reactivity.

articles.title: Twin Analyses Without Zygosity Information: The Promise and Pitfalls of the Normal Finite-Mixture-Distribution Twin Model
articles.authors: Madelief Isabel Kuijper; Camiel M. van der Laan; Abe Dirk Hofman; Elsje van Bergen; Bruno Sauce
articles.abstract: Twin designs offer a powerful framework for disentangling genetic and environmental sources of individual differences, but require zygosity information that is often unavailable. When zygosity is unknown, analyses may still be possible using a normal finite-mixture-distribution model. This model assumes that the bivariate twin phenotype is normally distributed within each zygosity group, an assumption that cannot be directly verified because zygosity is unobserved. We conducted a simulation study to determine how well the normal finite-mixture-distribution model recovered parameters if this assumption is violated and if the twin difference distribution could serve as a diagnostic for violations. In univariate and bivariate simulations, we varied sample size, variance structure and the degree and source of kurtosis. We examined whether marginal score transformations reduce bias. We applied univariate and bivariate models to educational gaming data with unknown zygosity. Simulation estimates were most precise at high heritability and remained reasonably stable with samples as small as 200 pairs. Excess kurtosis in twin difference scores substantially biased estimates, and marginal transformations did not meaningfully reduce bias. Kurtosis-related bias was smaller in the bivariate model than in the univariate model. Empirical estimates based on the univariate model seemed highly biased, producing very high heritability estimates (0.91–0.99), except for the measure of division-skills (A = 0.58, C= 0, E= 0.42). Estimates based on the bivariate model seemed less biased, but unstable. Although mixture modelling may be used for twin-analyses without zygosity information, violations of bivariate normality may introduce considerable, difficult-to-detect bias, warranting careful interpretation of results.

articles.title: Cannabis Use Disorder and Depression: A within-network resting state functional connectivity study
articles.authors: Nora de Bode; Emese Kroon; Jia Hua Hsieh; Francesca Filbey; Janna Cousijn
articles.abstract:
Background: Cannabis Use Disorder (CUD) and comorbid depressive symptoms are a prevalent mental health concern. However, knowledge about potentially shared underlying neurobiological mechanisms remains mixed. Therefore, this study systematically investigated the role of depressive symptoms in the relationship between CUD, cannabis use characteristics, and within-network resting state functional connectivity (RSFC) in multiple networks implicated in both CUD and depression: the Executive Control Network (ECN), the Salience Network (SN), the Default Mode Network (DMN), and the Basal Ganglia Network (BGN).
Methods: In 152 participants with CUD and 114 controls, we examined the effects of group (CUD versus control), depressive symptoms, and their interaction on within-network RSFC. Within the CUD group, we further assessed the effects of depression diagnosis, cannabis use characteristics, and their interaction with depressive symptoms on within-network RSFC. We also tested associations between behavioral measures of depression and cannabis use. Bayesian analyses quantified the strength of all evidence.
Results: Although symptoms of depression were associated with CUD severity, there were no direct effects of depressive symptoms, nor any interaction effects between depressive symptoms, CUD status, or other cannabis use characteristics on within-network RSFC. In most networks, exploratory Bayesian analyses provided moderate to strong evidence in favor of the null hypotheses. 
Conclusion: The absence of significant group differences in within-network RSFC suggests it is not a robust marker in samples characterized by having a CUD and comorbid depressive symptoms, compared to controls. Instead, potentially individual characteristics, such as greater depression severity and heaviness of use, may contribute to altered RSFC.

articles.title: Purpose in life and markers of affective, behavioral, cognitive, and relational engagement measured across moments in daily life
articles.authors: Angelina Sutin; Martina Luchetti; Antonio Terracciano
articles.abstract: Engagement is a key factor in models of both purpose in life and cognitive health. These models usually measure engagement as participation in specific activities, whereas models of education and employment operationalize engagement as having specific affective, behavioral, cognitive, and relational components. As a step toward a more comprehensive model of purpose and cognitive health in middle and older adulthood, this research examines the association between dispositional purpose and metrics of these components of engagement reported across moments in daily life using ecological momentary assessment methodology. Participants (N=2577; Mage=63.52; SD=11.24) reported on their momentary engagement three times a day for eight days (>48,000 momentary assessments; 79% compliance). Higher dispositional purpose in life was associated with greater affective (e.g., positive affect; b=11.49 [SE=.61]), behavioral (e.g., persistent; b=10.20 [SE=.48]), cognitive (e.g., attentive; b=8.99 [SE=.46]), and relational (e.g., feeling connected; b=7.98 [SE=.57]) engagement in daily life (ps<.001). These associations were not moderated by sociodemographic factors. Higher purpose was mostly unrelated to variability in these metrics accounting for mean engagement: Participants higher in purpose had less variability because their average engagement was high. Greater engagement may be a mechanism in the pathway between purpose in life and the better health outcomes associated with it.

articles.title: Political mental health is distinct from mental health and has unique political correlates
articles.authors: Delaney Peterson; Gijs Schumacher; Frederic R. Hopp; Bert N. Bakker
articles.abstract: Politics is associated with a range of negative mental and physical health problems. Some work considers how  MH disorders are associated with politics. Others, study to what extent there are mental ill-health symptoms people attribute to politics. We contend directly with why a focus on mental (ill) health attributed to politics is necessary in the study of MH and politics and examine 1) to what extent measures specific to the domain of politics are conceptually distinct from general measures and 2) to what extent domain-specific measures demonstrate a different pattern of association to political correlates. We test and compare the construct and predictive validity of "political mental health" (PMH) and MH in two pre-registered samples in the Netherlands. We find PMH is uni-dimensional and conceptually distinct, but related, to MH and that PMH has stronger associations to relevant political correlates -such as conspiracy mentality and news aversion- and explains unique variance above and beyond a MH measure. We end with suggestions for future work on MH and politics that include the use domain-specific PMH and conclude that PMH has both clinical and political implications.

articles.title: Art as a Bridge Between Brains? A Study of Artist and Viewer Emotion Communication in Abstract Drawing using fNIRS Hyperscanning
articles.authors: Corinna Kühnapfel; Theresa Rahel Demmer; Tao Liu; Helmut Leder; Matthew Pelowski
articles.abstract: Art has often been suggested to evoke shared emotional and empathetic connections between artists and their recipients. However, to-date, there is little empirical research on whether emotions are actually transmitted through art, especially between viewers and artists. Even rarer: can art actually "bridge" both parties' brains? We introduced a paradigm assessing inter-brain synchrony between artists and viewers via functional Near Infrared Spectroscopy (fNIRS) hyperscanning. A cohort of 19 non-artistically trained participants made four abstract drawings, each expressing one target emotion (happiness, pride, fear, anger). The drawing process was filmed and brain activity recorded over affective and cognitive empathy/theory of mind-related cortical regions. A sub-set of the resulting videos (two by each artist, balanced for positive/negative valence) were then viewed by a new cohort of (N = 28) participants while their own brain activity was measured over the same regions and with the task of identifying the target emotion and rating the quality and expressivity of the drawings and difficulty of the recognition task. Behavioral results suggested that all viewers could recognize intended emotions above chance, although with inter-viewer and inter-artwork/artist differences. At the intra-brain level, we found a significant relation between viewers' successful emotion recognition and lower activity in their rIFG as well as between viewers' subjective ratings of task difficulty and drawing expressiveness and activities in viewers' and artists' mPFC and IPL. At the inter-brain level, we found a notable relation between higher coherence between both brains over bilateral lIPL regions and viewers' ratings of lower subjective difficulty in the task. We also detected a positive relation between the artists' ratings of the expressivity of their artworks and higher inter-brain coherence over the rIFG and ATL areas, providing first evidence for an artwork to indeed lead to shared activity patterns between brains.

articles.title: Exploring Domain-Specific Pathways to Resilience in Adolescents: The Role of Executive Functioning
articles.authors: Ranjani Nadarajan; Guillaume Elgbeili; Irina Pokhvisneva; Justin Russotti; Hannah Swerbenski; Patricia P Silveira; Kieran J. O’Donnell; Jennie G. Noll; Ian Gotlib; Michael J Meaney
articles.abstract:
Objective:
Despite the significant risks associated with early life adversity (ELA), there is considerable individual variation in functioning, suggesting the operation of resilience mechanisms. We propose that executive functioning (EF) contributes to resilience following early life adversity (ELA); in this study we examined the mediating role of EF in the associations between ELA and body mass index (BMI), and psychological and academic functioning. 

Method: 
Leveraging data from adolescent girls in the High Adversity (n = 316) and Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD; n = 2533) cohorts, we examined whether total EF problems mediated the associations between ELA and the three outcomes. We then investigated if the two EF indices, Behavioural Regulation and Metacognition differentially mediated the associations between ELA and the three outcomes. 

Results: 
Total EF problems mediated the associations between ELA and BMI z-scores, emotional/behavioural problems, and academic performance in both cohorts. In the High Adversity cohort, both EF indices (Behavioural Regulation and Metacognition) significantly mediated associations between ELA and emotional/behavioural problems and academic performance, but not BMI z-scores. In the ABCD cohort, the Metacognition Index significantly mediated the associations between ELA and BMI z-scores, emotional/behavioural problems and academic performance, whereas the Behavioural Regulation Index mediated only the association between ELA and emotional/behavioural problems.

Conclusion:
Findings indicate that EF may be a potent intervention target for promoting resilience in broad populations.

articles.title: Longitudinal event‑sampling dataset of football fans across five countries during the 2024 European championship
articles.authors: Hannes M. Petrowsky; Yannik A. Escher; Lea Boecker; Kathi Diel; NA; Meikel Neumann; Danna Oomen; Leonie Schwarz; Marie-Annik Strauch; Marcel Weber
articles.abstract: This dataset was collected during the UEFA EURO football championship in 2024. It provides match-contingent experience sampling data, including (a) individual-level variables such as life satisfaction, (b) interpersonal variables such as prosocial intent, and (c) broader societal variables such as political trust. The data points are nested in a sample of N = 1,012 individuals from five countries (England, Germany, Italy, the Netherlands, Poland). Using an event-sampling method with four to eight measurement waves (depending on how far the national team progressed in the tournament), we captured time-sensitive fluctuations in a total of m = 30 variables in response to the national team’s objective (i.e., win, draw, loss) and perceived success. In addition, the dataset contains moderating variables including Big-5 personality traits, conspiracy and free-will beliefs, or alcohol consumption and betting behaviors, and detailed match-specific data (e.g., shots on target, yellow cards, attendance). Our data descriptor portrays the methodological approach, data collection procedures, and available variables, and outlines how the data may be used for future research.

articles.title: Shared Human Symbolic Cognition Across Time and Culture
articles.authors: Yuxi Chu; Xingcan Chen; Yanchao Bi
articles.abstract: Humans uniquely externalize ideas through abstract visual symbols. Although symbols are ubiquitous across human cultures and history, the cognitive basis of symbolic meaning remains unclear. We tested whether human symbolic interpretation is supported by shared cognitive tendencies that allow convergence across individuals, cultures, and historical periods without shared conventions. We collected 40,416 open-ended interpretations from 1,637 participants in China and the United States for 242 historical symbols spanning different cultural contexts and over 40,000 years. Participants converged on similar interpretations, with convergence predicting independently established meanings. This effect generalized across cultures and historical periods. In contrast, vision models trained on natural images failed to reproduce human interpretations, indicating that symbolic understanding cannot be reduced to general-purpose visual capacities. Instead, humans appear to rely on stable cognitive principles that map abstract forms onto conceptual representations. These results help explain how symbols emerge, stabilize, and persist across generations.

articles.title: Effects of Ultra-short Offline States on Item-level Verbal Memory
articles.authors: Erin J. Wamsley; Justin Bret Barron; Will Livingston
articles.abstract:
Memory is consolidated during “offline” states of reduced attention to the external stimulus environment, including sleep and resting wakefulness. While consolidation is usually studied on a timescale of hours to days, accumulating evidence suggests that organisms also transiently go offline for very short durations during wakefulness, on a timescale of seconds. Here, we asked how going offline during the intertrial intervals of a verbal memory task affects memory. Participants encoded eight lists of 20 words, during which we continuously recorded high density EEG, pupillometry, RTs, and occasional reports of participants’ subjective focus of attention. Using these multimodal data, we applied our previously published methods to define whether participants were “online” or “offline” just prior to and following the time each individual word was presented. As in prior studies, we found evidence that participants fluctuated between three distinct states during the task, which we term “online” (high EEG delta and theta, larger pupil diameter), “offline1” (high EEG alpha, smaller pupil diameter), and “offline2” (high EEG slow oscillation, smaller pupil diameter). As
anticipated, encoding was superior when participants were online just prior to word presentation. But contrary to our hypotheses, entering an offline state just after word presentation did not improve memory. These observations are discussed in light of memory consolidation theory, including recent evidence of “micro-offline” consolidation in the motor domain.

articles.title: Determining Learner-Specific Prompt Type and Prompt-Fading Procedures for Adults with Autism Spectrum Disorder
articles.authors: Ashley R. Kemmerer; Jason Vladescu; Tina M. Sidener; Ruth DeBar; Marlesha C. Bell
articles.abstract: It is widely recognized that individuals with autism spectrum disorder (ASD) often require individualized educational programming. However, only a few studies have evaluated using an assessment to identify the most efficient and effective instructional components for individual learners. We replicated and extended Seaver and Bourret (2014) by conducting prompt-type and prompt-fading assessments with two adults with ASD to identify the most efficient prompt type (model, vocal-gestural, or full physical) and prompt-fading procedure (progressive prompt delay, most-to-least, or least-to-most). Following these assessments, we compared the most and least efficient procedures while evaluating whether the assessment results generalized to additional skill types. We also compared the assessment results to those of a decision-making tool, the Systematic Worksheet for the Evaluation of Effective Prompting Strategies (SWEEPS; Cowan et al., 2023). The most efficient prompt type was learner-specific, whereas both participants learned most efficiently using a progressive prompt delay. These outcomes were replicated across additional functional skills and showed clear differences between the most and least efficient procedures. The assessment results were also consistent with the SWEEPS.

articles.title: Pregnant Preconceptions: Experiences of Social Monitoring during Pregnancy
articles.authors: Michelle Ann Kline; Lora Adair; Kenzie Hanson; Heidi Colleran
articles.abstract: Pregnancy is highly visible, and subject to gendered, classed, and racialised expectations. It is also characterised by frequently changing health and wellness recommendations, and cultural narratives regarding 'perfect motherhood'. This paper explores the role of social monitoring—defined as intervention or interruption during daily activities to influence behaviour—on the experience of pregnancy. This article examines who engages in social monitoring; on what topics; how these are valanced (hostile/ benevolent sexism); style of delivery (e.g., advice, help, or constraint) and targets (e.g., race/ethnicity, age, or marital status of the pregnant person); and how pregnant people respond. Participants (n = 205, M age = 37, SD age = 10) were recruited through social media and Prolific, were English-speaking and had experienced at least 12 weeks of pregnancy. Responses were deductively coded using nVivo. We find that socially senior women (mothers, mothers-in-law) are frequently named as intervenors, and that easily observable behaviours (e.g., exercise, food and drink, body or weight) are frequent topics of intervention. While the most common response to monitoring was to persist rather than change their behaviour, some respondents described withdrawing to avoid further intervention. Interventions contain both hostile and benevolent sexism, with Black women reporting a higher ratio of hostility than did other race/ethnic groups. These findings have implications for the social connectedness and mental health of pregnant people and highlight the role of shame and judgment in creating 'no win' situations for mothers-to-be.

articles.title: Do depression stigma and literacy differ between military personnel and the general population? Evidence from a matched comparison of the German Armed Forces (Bundeswehr) and the general population
articles.authors: Caroline Allenhof; Hanna Reich; Andreas Czaplicki; Anna-Lena Nadine Jasmin Leimberger; Jil Zippelius; Annika Heer; Ulrich Hegerl; Gerd Dieter Willmund
articles.abstract:
Objective: Military personnel (MP) are at increased risk of mental health problems yet help-seeking remains low. Depression stigma and literacy are potential barriers to help-seeking, but comparative data between MP and the general population (GP) are limited. This study compared depression stigma and literacy between Bundeswehr MP and the GP and examined sociodemographic and occupational correlates of stigma and literacy.
Methods: Baseline data from operational military personnel (OMP) and military university affiliates (MUA, combined n = 1,964) were compared with participants from representative GP surveys (n = 15,535) matched using propensity scores for age, gender, and education. Depression stigma and literacy were assessed using validated scales and analyzed with multiple regression.
Results:  OMP reported lower personal and perceived stigma than the GP (p ≤ .030), whereas MUA did not differ from the GP in stigma, and neither military group differed from the GP in depression literacy. Mid-aged MP showed lower depression literacy (p < .05). Lower education and lack of contact with depression were associated with higher personal stigma and lower depression literacy in both MP and GP. Personal experience with depression was associated with higher perceived stigma, whereas contact via family/friends/work was associated with higher depression literacy (p ≤ .019). Within the military, lower-ranked and never-deployed personnel reported higher personal stigma (p ≤ .006) and lower literacy (p = .047).
Conclusions: Depression stigma was not higher among MP than in the GP. Interventions should target mid-aged, lower-ranked, and never-deployed personnel, as well as lower-educated individuals without depression contact.

articles.title: Academic dishonesty and questionable research practices in final thesis writing
articles.authors: Lenka Vargová; Marcel Martončik; Gabriel Baník; Artem Buts; Pavol Kačmár; Matus Adamkovic
articles.abstract: Final theses represent a substantial but relatively overlooked form of academic knowledge production. This study provides a descriptive and exploratory insight into academic dishonesty and questionable research practices in thesis writing among N = 1,007 Slovak higher-education graduates who defended a bachelor’s, master’s, rigorous, or doctoral thesis between 2012 and 2024. Participants reported on thesis characteristics, supervision, institutional expectations, psychological and contextual factors, and engagement in dishonest practices such as plagiarism, blind referencing, selective reporting, HARKing, ghostwriting, and data manipulation and fabrication. Although participants generally reported high ethical standards and low intentions to cheat, self-reported engagement in several dishonest practices was not negligible. Academic dishonesty was most consistently associated with neutralizing attitudes, more lenient moral standards, and external pressure, whereas thesis-related characteristics played a comparatively minor role. The findings suggest that academic dishonesty in thesis writing is a complex and multifaceted phenomenon shaped by both individual justifications and contextual pressures. Effective prevention requires a multilevel strategy that simultaneously addresses malleable contextual factors such as supervision, institutional expectations, and peer norms, together with the gradual formation of ethical attitudes and moral identity.

articles.title: A Sequence Sensitive Model of Encoding Precision
articles.authors: Michael Aristodemou; José C. García Alanis; Anna-Lena Schubert; Rogier Kievit
articles.abstract: Canonical visual encoding models do not incorporate the temporal structure of tasks, despite memory performance in real-life contexts almost invariably operating within sequentially structured activities. To address this gap, we developed a Sequence Sensitive model of visual encoding and compared its ability to explain the structure of fluctuations in encoding precision and recognition speed to two established resource-based models of visual encoding, the Population Coding model and the Variable Precision model. We use Dynamic Structural Equation Modeling to formalize the dynamic relationship between a neural proxy of encoding precision and recognition speed, as predicted by all three models. We compared the three resource-based models by fitting them to data from a large sample of 142 participants who completed 100 trials of a short-term memory task, the Sternberg task, while their neural activity was recorded using an electroencephalogram. Our results show that the Sequence Sensitive model outperforms canonical candidates in the context of understanding performance on sequential trial tasks. However, a visual comparison of the model-implied and observed values shows that the three resource-based models only explain a moderate portion of the observed variance. Thus, we should aim to integrate sequential effects into richer and more comprehensive models that incorporate additional features of working memory, such as the interaction between encoding and retrieval processes.

articles.title: Voices in the head: A population-level map of inner-speech modes and agency
articles.authors: Keisuke Suzuki; Hideyuki Takahashi
articles.abstract: Inner-speech research documents striking individual diversity through descriptive phenomenology, self-report questionnaires, and extreme phenotypes, yet prior studies typically sample a few hundred respondents, and no design has mapped how the major modes co-occur or trade off at population scale. We addressed this gap with a video-based online survey (clean N = 5,289): respondents rated seven inner-speech types across three scenarios, alongside agency and sensory-modality self-reports; to our knowledge, it is the largest survey to map this co-occurrence. The study is exploratory: we chart the space's shape rather than test effects, and that shape is continuous and voice-led. The 21-dimensional rating space is a continuous mixture rather than discrete categories, organized into a text family and a voice pair with own voice and visual imagery as standalone axes; reproducible profiles emerge only in the lower-dimensional sensory-modality subspace. The principal opposition is own voice versus visual imagery, not text versus imagery, refining dual-coding theory: only the phonological half of the verbal code opposes imagery, while the orthographic half is near-orthogonal. Own voice also forms an axis independent of the other-voice pair, and the two carry opposite-signed agency associations that are small, as expected for a single item, but consistent in sign. Because the agency and voice items share self/other wording, we treat continuity with sub-clinical voice-hearing as a hypothesis. Unsupervised topic modeling of 1,903 free-text descriptions further corroborates this voice-led organization. These coordinates provide a reference map for idiographic and clinical studies of inner-voice experience.

articles.title: From Clusters to Compositions: Fixed-Frame Archetypal Analysis of the Big Five
articles.authors: David Midgley; Jason Rentfrow; Jeff Potter; Samuel D. Gosling
articles.abstract:
Person-centered psychology has primarily used exploratory discovery algorithms, such as latent
profile analysis and cluster analysis, to identify population subgroups (e.g., “Resilient”). These
methods face several well-documented limitations: identified profiles vary across datasets
(sample dependence), the number of profiles relies on ambiguous stopping rules, and forcing
continuous personality configurations into discrete categories can conflate the measurement
granularity of Likert lattices with genuine structure. In response, we introduce fixed-frame
archetypal analysis (FFAA), which represents each individual as a continuous compositional
mixture relative to fixed theoretical extrema (the absolute corners of the Big Five hypercube)
using closed-form multilinear interpolation. The method contributes at two levels. For the
individual respondent, FFAA is a lossless reparameterization of trait scores into a fixed, sample-
independent coordinate system, providing (1) fixed profile definitions, (2) high-resolution
measurement of configural differences, and (3) computational scalability. Across respondents,
because compositions are nonlinear functions of the traits, averaging them encodes the
covariation among traits that marginal averages do not retain. We illustrate the individual-level
properties through simulation studies and establish a normative baseline from over 10 million
respondents; at the aggregate level, these data characterize the maturity principle across the adult
lifespan not as discrete stages but as a continuous geometric migration that is broadly similar
across regions. We propose that moving from “profile discovery” to “compositional
measurement” offers a mathematically grounded approach to person-centered psychology,
though establishing predictive validity and extending FFAA to other personality measures remain
important next steps.

articles.title: Mental Health Clinicians' Knowledge, Attitudes, Practices and Barriers regarding Diet's Effects on Depression: a Systematic Review of Observational Studies
articles.authors: David Morgan; James Sorensen; Arriel Vaz; Tetyana Rocks; Delyse Tien; Leo Chen; Heidi Staudacher
articles.abstract: Depression is a leading cause of disease burden worldwide with significant interest in new therapeutic approaches. Nutritional psychiatry is emerging as an increasingly evidence-based therapeutic tool. However, its incorporation into mainstream clinical practice has lagged. Key to understanding and addressing this lag is the clinician’s experience, with few studies having explored this area to date. This systematic review sets out to identify and synthesise the available literature on mental health clinicians’ (MHCs’) knowledge, attitudes, practice and barriers (KAPB) regarding diet’s role in depression. A systematic search across 5 databases (MEDLINE, Emcare, PsycInfo, CINAHL and the Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews) was conducted on April 3rd, 2026. Eligible studies sampled MHCs working with adults and explored the KAPB domains relating to diet’s role in depression. 12 publications representing 10 unique studies from around the world were included. Results consistently found low levels of training but high interest in further education. Attitudes and practices varied across studies, ranging from scepticism to strong support, with there being a predominance of support in more recent studies. Clinicians incorporating nutritional psychiatry into practice often reported low confidence or perceived expertise. Recommended approaches included Mediterranean-style dietary patterns, adherence to national dietary guidelines, and nutritional supplements, most frequently vitamin D, folic acid, B vitamins and omega-3. The main reported barriers to practice included insufficient training, concerns about professional scope and doubts about client interest and adherence. Overall, few studies were eligible and many were at risk of selection and reporting bias. Together they nonetheless provided a useful initial overview of many key and relevant themes. Findings highlighted the need for greater integration of nutritional psychiatry into mental health education, professional development, and service delivery. Future efforts should focus on localised implementation studies and interventions addressing identified barriers to practice.

articles.title: Idiographic Memory Modelling in ACT-R Using Alternating Maximum Likelihood Estimation
articles.authors: Maarten van der Velde; Thomas Wilschut; Hedderik van Rijn; Andrea Stocco
articles.abstract: Performance on memory tasks varies systematically between individuals and within individuals over time. However, computational models of memory are often fitted at the group level or summarise performance in a single parameter, potentially obscuring the distinct cognitive processes that give rise to this variation. Here we present a likelihood-based procedure for fitting the multi-parameter ACT-R declarative memory model at the level of the individual. Using this method, we jointly estimate five participant-level or idiographic parameters, each capturing a distinct component of memory performance, together with item-level variation in memorability. A parameter recovery study on synthetic data shows that these participant- and item-level parameters are jointly identifiable from realistic amounts of data on a typical spaced repetition memory task, requiring only about 40-90 observations of response accuracy and speed per participant. We demonstrate the method on a dataset of individuals with mild cognitive impairment and age-matched healthy controls, finding that mild cognitive impairment is marked by differences in three parameters signifying accelerated forgetting, less efficient memory retrieval, and general psychomotor slowing. These results show that the method can successfully decompose observed performance on a memory task into separable, individually interpretable components. More broadly, this work contributes to the development of fine-grained idiographic memory models that can capture dynamics in memory performance both between and within individuals, with applications in experimental research and applied settings, such as computational phenotyping for clinical diagnosis.

articles.title: chatPsych: An Artificial Intelligence Interface for Human-AI Interaction Research
articles.authors: Oliver Lack; Carolyn Semmler
articles.abstract: Human computer interaction research remains largely ungeneralisable and popular methods fail to adequately represent the rapidly evolving systems in society. chatPsych aims to provide the research community with access to a generalisable method that facilitates rich, representative human-AI interaction contexts. chatPsych is a novel research tool for building and deploying studies that focus on interaction with frontier AI models. The system comprises programmable surveys surrounding interactions with a chat/assistant interface that connects participants to a specified model with researcher tailored parameters. The aesthetics and features of the interface resemble prevalent chat/assistant technologies and data from interactions/surveys are scraped and locally downloadable from the researcher dashboard GUI. This GUI also allows programming and randomisation of varying AI agents to participant interactions. These agents can be modified via prompt engineering, model hyperparameters, model selection, moderated responses/prompts/captures/buttons, and more. Although chatPsych may be adapted by technically inclined investigators, it offers extensive features and deployment instructions for non-technical researchers. The system uses standardised API connections to an extensive range of model providers. Its standalone nature provides accessibility/customisability for many study designs at scale online and in lab-based settings. We introduce and validate this tool as an open-source codebase for the research community. chatPsych is available at https://chatpsych.org/.

articles.title: Support for climate and biodiversity policies differs across cultural worldviews in Japan
articles.authors: Aya Takagi; Ryosuke Nakadai
articles.abstract: Social polarisation poses a major challenge to collective climate action. In this context, biodiversity conservation, when aligned with climate objectives, may face less worldview-driven resistance than direct climate policies. However, the links between values of nature and wider cultural worldviews remain poorly understood. Here we show, using a nationwide survey (N = 1,133) in Japan, how cultural worldviews relate to risk perception and environmental policy support.  Individuals with hierarchical worldviews perceived climate change risks as lower and tended to be less supportive of climate change measures than individuals with more egalitarian worldviews. Yet these negative associations were weaker for perceived biodiversity loss risks and for support for climate change-related biodiversity conservation measures, and no direct association was found for stand-alone biodiversity conservation measures. General scientific knowledge was positively associated with support for biodiversity conservation measures, including those related to climate change. These associations between cultural worldviews and policy support differed across climate and biodiversity policy domains.

articles.title: Exploring the role of identity fusion and group identification in predicting parochialism amongst Indonesian Islamic groups
articles.authors: Christopher Michael Kavanagh; Susilo Wibisono; Rohan Kapitány; Whinda Yustisia; Idhamsyah Eka Putra; Harvey Whitehouse
articles.abstract: Indonesia is home to more Muslims than any other country in the world, yet the interpretation of the faith differs between groups, communities, and individuals. Here we examine how the diversity of beliefs and practices among Indonesian Muslims relate to group bonding and parochialism. Specifically, we examine the predictive power of two distinct types of group alignment, group identification and identity fusion, among politically unaﬀiliated Indonesian Muslims and individuals associated with two prominent Islamic organizations: the explicitly political and Islamist Prosperity and Justice Party (PKS) and the more moderate Nahdlatul Ulama (NU), the largest religious organization in Indonesia. We found that PKS members were more fused to relevant group identities than NU members or unaﬀiliated Indonesian Muslims. Across all three groups, identity fusion was associated with parochial outcomes, including expressed willingness to carry out extreme pro-group actions, but group identification was the stronger predictor. We discuss how religious beliefs and practices shape parochial attitudes, as well as the implications for theoretical models linking fusion, identification, threat, and extreme behaviour.

articles.title: Visual Pattern Completion of Occluded Objects Promotes Robust Visual Learning
articles.authors: Jennifer Gove; David E. Huber; Rosemary Cowell
articles.abstract: Recognizing a partially occluded object is effortless and automatic: visual cortex fills in the missing parts to infer a recognizable whole. We tested whether part-to-whole visual pattern completion constitutes not just a retrieval operation, but also a learning mechanism. Participants studied whole objects, after which each object was assigned either to retrieval practice, cued by a circular aperture offering a “peephole” view, or to restudy. At final test, retrieval-practiced (peephole-cued) objects were better remembered than restudied objects – a visual testing effect. The task eliminated the utility of conceptual information, suggesting that this testing effect occurs without semantic mediation, contrary to prominent theories. Critically, when retrieval no longer required pattern completion—retrieval without a peephole cue—the testing effect disappeared. Thus, we report a non-semantically mediated visual testing effect driven by occlusion-induced pattern completion. We suggest that visual pattern completion supports not only occluded object recognition, but also robust visual learning.

articles.title: Spatiotemporal cognitive modes underlying theory of mind: A Human Connectome Project fMRI study
articles.authors: Ava Momeni; Maddie Evora; Karanvir Gill; Amber Lu; Todd Stephen Woodward
articles.abstract: Theory of mind (ToM), or mentalization, is a core aspect of social cognition that enables individuals to infer the mental states of others. Brain activity underlying ToM has been extensively studied using functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI), with a primary focus on the Default Mode (DM). We investigated the interplay of multiple cognitive modes (i.e., cognitive processes eliciting distinct fMRI-derived spatiotemporal patterns) engaged during ToM, extending beyond but still including the DM. We analyzed a large-scale fMRI dataset from the Human Connectome Project Social (HCP) Cognition task, where participants (n=500) evaluated whether movements of animated shapes depicted social or random interactions. Six cognitive modes were retrieved using constrained principal component analysis (CPCA): (1) Multiple Demand (MD), (2) Default Mode B (DM-B), (3) Auditory Attention for Response (AAR), (4) Focus on Visual Features (FVF), (5) Language (LAN), and (6) Re-Evaluation (RE-EV). The functions of these cognitive modes have been characterized based on analyses of their task-induced blood-oxygen-level-dependent (BOLD) changes over many tasks, but in the context of the HCP Social Cognition task, their BOLD changes were interpreted as: (1) mentally projecting into social narratives (DM-B), (2) allocating external attention to task-relevant components (MD), (3) processing non-verbal communication (LAN), (4) focusing on visual features of the social stimuli (FVF), (5) suppressing auditory attention due to increased visual attention (AAR), and (6) re-evaluating for possibly missed social patterns (RE-EV). Thus, our results confirmed the activation of DM during ToM, but also specified the contributions of five additional cognitive modes. An application of this work is that the six sets of mode-aligned functional patterns can serve as separate inputs for machine learning algorithms aimed at subtyping patients with psychiatric conditions to advance toward precision psychiatry.

articles.title: Serial dependence is unchanged by confirmatory but reversed by error feedback
articles.authors: Yael Shamir-Bercovich; Shir Angel; Aihua Chen; ADAM ZAIDEL
articles.abstract: Perceptual decisions are shaped not only by current sensory input but also by recent experience. Serial dependence exposes an internal brain process that biases current decisions toward previous ones, even in the absence of feedback about their correctness. When available, feedback on one’s choices is an external factor that also shapes subsequent decisions. However, it remains unknown how these distinct history-dependent effects – of internal and external origin – compare, and how they vary across individuals and modalities. Here, we compared the effects of serial dependence with or without trial-by-trial feedback on perceptual decisions regarding vestibular or visual self-motion stimuli, and examined how they relate to autistic traits. Seventy healthy adults performed a two-alternative forced-choice heading discrimination task with visual or vestibular cues (separately), under two conditions: with feedback indicating whether each choice was correct or incorrect, and without feedback. Without feedback, significant choice repetition was seen in both modalities. Surprisingly, on average correct-choice feedback did not enhance the choice repetition effect; rather it remained similar in magnitude. By contrast, incorrect-choice feedback led to a large, opposite effect – choice switching – which was stronger for vestibular cues. Thus, only error signals, not confirmatory signals, modulated serial dependence. Choice repetition in the no-feedback vestibular condition correlated significantly with autistic traits. This relationship was not observed when feedback was provided. The similarity in choice repetition following correct-choice feedback vs. no feedback was more pronounced in individuals with high autism traits, and suggests that positive serial dependence (without feedback) may reflect an internal assumption that one’s decisions were mostly correct.

articles.title: Ingressing Minds: Causal Patterns Beyond Genetics and Environment in Natural, Synthetic, and Hybrid Embodiments
articles.authors: Michael Levin
articles.abstract: I argue that the emerging sciences of synthetic morphology and diverse intelligence suggest non-physicalist models of mind and show how they can be empirically investigated. Whence the anatomical, physiological, molecular-biological, and behavioral properties of engineered new beings that have never before existed, and do not have a history of selection? Understanding, predicting, and guiding new forms of life and mind requires characterizing a structured latent space of patterns. Developmental, synthetic, and behavioral biology should take seriously, and exploit, the kinds of non-physicalist ideas that are already a staple of Platonist mathematics. I propose the following hypotheses. (1) Patterns in this space span a highly variable degree of agency, comprising a spectrum ranging from static truths studied by mathematicians to active ones studied by behavioral scientists (i.e., some patterns on the same spectrum as mathematical truths are kinds of minds). (2) The relationship between mind and body is the same as the relationship between causally instructive mathematical facts and physics. (3) Living beings have no monopoly on the “free lunches” provided by the ingression of these patterns into the physical world. While traditional computationalist views of living and cognitive systems are insufficient, my framework erases artificial distinctions between organisms and machines, framing all physical constructs (natural or engineered) as being, to various degrees, in-formed by patterns from the latent space. I sketch a research program, already begun, inspired by these ideas. Such frameworks, while contradicting long-held assumptions of both mechanists and organicists, could have many implications for evolutionary biology, regenerative medicine, AI, and the ethics of synthbiosis with the forthcoming immense diversity of morally important beings.

articles.title: Parallel Claustral Networks for Behavioral Control in Health and Disease
articles.authors: Ami Citri; Andreas B. Wulff; Christian Faig; Huriye Atilgan; Kenichiro Negishi; Solange P. Brown; Brian Mathur; George J. Augustine; Yavin Shaham
articles.abstract:
The claustrum is among the most highly interconnected structures in the mammalian brain, with reciprocal projections spanning the cortical mantle and a growing roster of functions that range from sleep regulation to attentional control. 

Here we review current understanding of claustral circuit organization and function, synthesizing findings from anatomical, physiological, computational, and behavioral studies. We argue that a unifying logic underlies the apparent diversity of claustral functions: the claustrum operates through projection-defined subnetworks whose input selectivity, output targets, and local circuit interactions collectively determine their functional contribution, and whose impact on cortical targets varies with arousal and ongoing brain state. 

Corticoclaustral connectivity is organized into matched input-output modules, local inhibitory circuits confer spatial filtering properties that amplify strong cortical signals while suppressing weaker ones, and claustral output populations differ in their laminar targets, postsynaptic cell-type preferences, and molecular properties, producing diverse and context-dependent effects on cortical dynamics. 

These circuit properties translate into claustral regulation of cortical population dynamics across multiple timescales, from the moment-to-moment coordination of brain states to the consolidation of frontal control strategies. In pain and addiction, specific claustral projection populations are selectively dysregulated, and distinct subnetworks exert opposing influences on nociceptive sensitivity and drug-related behavior according to their cortical targets. 

Together, these findings position the claustrum as a modular subcortical hub for the selective regulation of distributed cortical networks and identify it as a candidate therapeutic target for disorders characterized by dysregulated cortical network activity, including chronic pain, addiction, and impulsivity.

articles.title: Visual preferences across species: a comparative framework
articles.authors: Esther Ureña; Marcos Nadal
articles.abstract: Human preferences for visual features such as curvature, visual structure, biological motion, and color have rarely been studied from a comparative perspective, and are often interpreted as reflecting distinctively human aesthetic processes. We review evidence from primates, birds, fish, and insects showing that comparable preferences are widespread across animal species. These preferences often emerge early, sometimes without prior exposure, but remain highly flexible: they are shaped by developmental trajectories, experience, behavioral goals, multisensory input, physiological state, and ecological context. Mechanistic evidence suggests that these preferences emerge from interactions between species-specific sensory tuning and higher-order neural systems involved in attention, valuation, and action. These preferences help orient behavior toward ecologically relevant features of the environment, supporting foraging, oviposition, social attachment, and social learning. Together, these findings challenge the view that human visual preferences reflect uniquely human aesthetic processes. Instead, they suggest that such preferences are better understood as flexible outputs of perceptual and motivational systems that are widely distributed across species and have been reshaped by human evolution, development, and culture.

articles.title: Spectral change or Jensen gap? Log-ratio baseline correction for time-frequency M/EEG is negatively biased
articles.authors: Isaac Kinley; Reece Pierre Roberts; Jed Meltzer; Donna Rose Addis
articles.abstract:
Background: Time-frequency M/EEG analysis generally involves baseline correction to measure spectral changes following events of interest. The decibel or ``log-ratio'' baseline correction method divides spectral power in a target time window by average baseline power and log-transforms this ratio. This approach has several desirable properties and is ubiquitously available in popular software tools. However, we show that due to the concavity of the logarithm function, it follows from Jensen's inequality that log-ratio baseline correction is negatively biased, falsely indicating a decrease in spectral power when none exists.
New Method: We propose an unbiased alternative to log-ratio correction in which mean log-transformed baseline power is subtracted from log-transformed power in a target time window. This ``mean-log-ratio'' approach preserves the advantages of log-ratio correction without bias is easily implemented.
Results: Using real and simulated data, we find that log-ratio baseline correction can cause true increases in spectral power to be incorrectly measured as decreases. This downward bias is evident across software implementations of this correction method.
Comparison with existing methods: Whereas downward bias was evident for log-ratio baseline correction, simple mean-subtraction correction (in which mean baseline power is subtracted) was not biased. Similarly, our proposed mean-log-ratio method faithfully reflected true increases in spectral power.
Conclusions: We recommend against log-ratio baseline correction due to its negative bias. Even when analyses primarily examine differences between conditions rather than absolute spectral change from baseline, a biased baseline correction method can influence how results are interpreted. For researchers compelled by the advantages of log-ratio correction, we recommend mean-log-ratio correction.

articles.title: Sociocultural Cannabis Attitudes in Relation to Personality and Cannabis Use: A Bayesian Network Approach
articles.authors: Erin Prince; Janna Cousijn; Emese Kroon; Francesca Filbey
articles.abstract:
Objective: Risky cannabis use is shaped by a myriad of factors, including individual and environmental factors. Individual level factors such as personality traits have well documented links to increased risky cannabis use, but how they interact with cannabis context to influence cannabis use has not yet been examined. This study examined the influences of personality traits, cannabis attitudes, and cultural context on symptoms related to cannabis use disorder. Understanding this link is important because it can help explain why cannabis use patterns vary across people and settings in service of more precise prevention and intervention strategies. 
Method: Fifty-eight adults from the U.S. and 81 adults from the Netherlands completed the Cannabis Use Disorder Identification Test (CUDIT), NEO Five Factor Inventory (NEO-FFI), and Cannabis Culture Questionnaire (CCQ). Bayesian network analyses were conducted to estimate conditional dependencies and probabilistic relationships among risky cannabis use, personality traits, and cannabis attitudes. 
Results: Personal and perceived peer positive cannabis attitudes formed a tightly interconnected cluster. Personality traits exhibited selective interconnections, with associations primarily occurring among personality domains rather than with cannabis attitudes or use severity. CUDIT scores had a direct negative association with conscientiousness. Legal context was the most highly connected node, linking cannabis use severity, positive cannabis attitudes, and multiple personality traits. 
Conclusions: These findings indicate that cannabis use severity is embedded within a broader network of individual factors that include personality and cannabis attitudes that may be shaped by contextual influences and provide support for culturally sensitive prevention and intervention efforts.

articles.title: Correlated motion information improves subsecond visual time perception
articles.authors: Anthony Alec Bruno; Leslie Welch
articles.abstract: Continuous visual information, like a conductor’s baton, is used to execute temporal behaviors like playing music with others. And yet, research in time perception has yielded the finding that time perception is superior in audition compared to vision. This finding depends on discrete stimuli, particularly visual flashes of light, which do not match how most temporal events unfold in the real world. We hypothesized that sub-second visual time perception would be improved by a moving stimulus relative to discrete flashes. We found that a continuous visual stimulus that resembled a bouncing ball dramatically improved time-interval discrimination performance over flashes. In subsequent experiments, we showed that the continuous visual stimulus improved performance over the discrete flashes because bounces are better events and because it had motion. When the correlation between the motion of the stimulus and the time intervals it depicted was intact, the motion provided extra temporal information that participants used to produce good performance. These results provide evidence that the asymmetry seen in the literature that time perception in audition is superior to vision is not universal. Continuous information from a moving stimulus can be exploited to produce good sub-second visual time perception.

articles.title: Myths About Sexual Violence in Online versus Offline Settings: Unveiling the Impact of the Cyberculture
articles.authors: Rocío Vizcaíno-Cuenca; Hugo Carretero-Dios; MONICA ROMERO SANCHEZ; Michael Eid
articles.abstract: Beliefs about sexual violence influence judgments and perpetration of violence against women. While offline sexual and intimate partner violence myths are well-studied, the rise of cyber-sexual violence raises questions about the specificity of related beliefs. Across two studies with Spanish- and English-speaking participants (N = 979), we examined the distinction and overlap among cyber-sexual violence myths, offline sexual violence myths, and offline intimate partner violence myths, and their unique role in predicting online victim blaming. Using confirmatory factor analyses, we compared different models—including unidimensional, correlated-factor, and bifactor-(S-1)—including later victim blaming as a criterion variable. Results indicated that cyber-sexual violence myths, offline sexual violence myths, and offline intimate partner violence myths are empirically distinct yet positively correlated, with moderate shared variance, especially between offline myths, reflecting a common ideological basis rooted in sexism. Crucially, cyber-sexual violence myths most strongly predicted online victim blaming, highlighting the need for interventions.

articles.title: Power output feedback improves cycling performance without affecting the perception of voluntariness in task failure
articles.authors: Juan José Pérez-Díaz; Daniel Sanabria; José-Antonio Salas-Montoro; Mikel Zabala
articles.abstract: The perception of voluntariness in task failure during extreme physical effort is debated in endurance sports. This study tested whether power meter data availability affects perceived voluntariness and performance in cyclists accustomed to power meters. Forty trained cyclists completed two counterbalanced maximal incremental tests: one without feedback and one with real-time power visualization. Confirmatory analyses showed that power visualization increased maximal aerobic power (MAP) without affecting VO2MAX, maximal heart rate, RPE, or perceived voluntariness. Exploratory analyses revealed an order effect: participants who first performed the test with power data perceived their decision to stop as more voluntary in that condition compared to the subsequent no-feedback test. This effect was absent in the reverse order group. No order effects were found for other variables. Cyclists’ performance judgments aligned with actual MAP differences. These findings show that access to power data enhances MAP.
