articles.abstract: Multimodel inference (such as multiverse analysis and many-analyst projects) is a family of sensitivity analyses that examines variation in results across alternative auxiliary assumptions of a research question. The observed variation in multimodel inferences has given rise to the idea that there is no single correct analysis, and that researchers should examine variability across a large range of plausible models instead. In this contribution, we critically reflect on the epistemological assumptions and consequences of this idea. Specifically, we argue that variation across alternative auxiliary assumptions can reveal uncertainty about how to validly test a hypothesis, but it cannot substitute for the epistemic warrant required to justify those assumptions. This is because, once a research question is translated into a specific empirical test by specifying the relevant auxiliary assumptions, there is a fact of the matter about which statistical analysis correctly implements that test and deviations from that analysis are not made epistemically acceptable merely by being included in a distribution. Thus, robustness across alternative sets of auxiliary assumptions with differing levels of justification does not, by itself, show that a theoretical claim is epistemically warranted. Researchers should invest in the empirical groundwork needed to identify valid tests of their hypotheses, rather than embracing variability across analyses and incorporating epistemic uncertainty into statistical inferences.

articles.title: Koshering Psychedelics: Ayahuasca in the Ultra-Orthodox (Haredi) Jewish World
articles.authors: Jonathan David; Aviva Berkovich-Ohana; ‪Yair Dor-Ziderman‬‏; Michael Fine; Hagit Bonny-Noach; Leor Roseman
articles.abstract: Background: Despite the rapid growth of psychedelic research, limited attention has been given to how psychedelics are used, experienced and integrated within highly conservative religious communities, and in Jewish contexts more broadly. This study addresses this gap by examining ayahuasca use among the ultra-Orthodox (Haredi) community, a highly religious, conservative, and collectivist Jewish group. Methods: This study used inductive thematic analysis of interviews with Haredi participants in Israel and the US (N = 23; age range: 21–60; 8 women), supplemented by participant observation and analysis of religious materials. Results: Six themes were identified: (a) settings: Haredi ayahuasca ceremonies were adapted to Jewish contexts; (b) motivations: use was primarily therapeutic; (c) acute ayahuasca experiences: experiences included distinct Jewish and Jewish mystical visionary content; (d) longer term religious effects: ayahuasca was linked to strengthened belief, connection to Judaism, and changes in religious practice; (e) religious tensions: included ayahuasca’s perceived foreignness to Haredi life, concerns about idolatry, mixed gender participation, and competing structures of authority (experience vs. text); and (f) strategies for addressing tensions: included medicalization, “koshering” ayahuasca’s set, setting, and experience (i.e., making them religiously permissible), and framing ceremonies as liminal spaces. Conclusions: This study broadens the scope of psychedelic science by examining ayahuasca use in a highly religious Orthodox context rarely included in the field. The findings show how ayahuasca settings, experiences, and long term effects are translated through Haredi and Jewish structures, involve religious tensions and resolutions, and highlight psychedelics’ contextual flexibility and continued diffusion into understudied populations.

articles.title: Heartbeat perception is dissociable into subjective detection and temporal judgement
articles.authors: Yusuke Haruki; Keisuke Suzuki; Yuri Terasawa; Kenji Ogawa; Olaf Blanke; Yuko Yotsumoto
articles.abstract: Heartbeat perception is widely studied as a behavioural index of interoception, yet different cardiac tasks share little variance, and what each task measures remains unclear. We developed the oneshot heartbeat detection task, in which each trial yields a subjective heartbeat detection response and, after a variable waiting interval, a cardiovisual synchrony judgement. As an exteroceptive control, the same trial structure was also implemented in a matched sound condition. We then asked how the two within-trial measures relate to each other and to conventional heartbeat counting scores obtained in the same sessions. Across three experiments (n = 43, 24, 21), subjective heartbeat detection on a given trial increased confidence in the synchrony judgement but not accuracy. Synchrony sensitivity remained stable across waiting intervals from 2 to 22 heartbeats. Cross-study analysis revealed that heartbeat counting performance was related to how frequently participants detected heartbeats and to their synchrony response criterion, but not to the synchrony sensitivity; in Experiment 1, the analogous indices in a matched sound condition showed no such relation. These data show that subjective detection and temporal judgement of heartbeats are dissociable components of heartbeat perception. We argue that weak convergence across cardiac paradigms may reflect this component structure rather than failures of measurement.

articles.title: The Development of Future-Self Concepts in Adolescence
articles.authors: Peggy Yin; Hal Hershfield
articles.abstract:
Mainstream media paints adolescents as moody, mean, and myopic in their decision-making and risk-taking, unable to act with their future selves in mind. Yet, adolescents may merely be sensing their future selves as distant and stranger-like, irrelevant to the goals and motivations of who they are now. This handbook chapter highlights the complex developmental processes through which young people form, refine, and prioritize their future selves. Drawing on a broad spectrum of interdisciplinary literature, we examine how adolescents aged 10 to 24 cultivate a
sense of continuity between their present and future selves, a key factor in long-term decision-making and overall well-being. Additionally, the chapter reviews innovative interventions, ranging from narrative exercises and role-play activities to immersive technologies like virtual reality and conversational AI, that help bridge the psychological gap between the present self and the future self. By synthesizing evidence from developmental psychology, neuroscience, human-computer interaction, and social psychology, it outlines promising directions for future research and practical applications aimed at empowering adolescents to make choices that promote sustained health, education, and financial stability.

articles.title: Menarche and ADHD: Associations Between Puberty, ADHD Traits, and Mental Health in Female Adolescents
articles.authors: Emily Friedel; Lisa Grech; David Skvarc; Caroline Gurvich; Emma McIntosh; Bethany Norton; Linda Baker; Cammy Griffin; Nandita Vijayakumar; Tim Silk
articles.abstract:
Background: Adolescents with attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) may be more vulnerable to mental health challenges during puberty, and some evidence suggests that puberty impacts ADHD traits. However, pubertal influences on adolescents assigned female at birth (AFAB) with ADHD are poorly understood. We investigated how menarche relates to ADHD traits, along with its moderating effects on mental health outcomes based on ADHD status or ADHD traits.    
Methods: This study used Adolescent Brain and Cognitive DevelopmentSM Study data from baseline to wave 3 (N = 5677; 100% female; baseline age M = 9.9, SD = 0.6; 51% non-Hispanic white). ADHD traits, internalising difficulties, and externalising difficulties were operationalised using the Child Behavior Checklist. Bayesian generalised linear mixed models were conducted to examine cross-sectional and longitudinal associations between menarche status and ADHD traits, along with moderating effects of menarche status on mental health according to ADHD status or traits. Comparative analyses were run with breast development. 
Results: Menarche status had a null association with concurrent ADHD traits, but post-menarche (versus pre-menarche) adolescents had higher ADHD traits the following year. Complete breast development (versus not started) predicted higher ADHD traits the next year. Menarche status and breast development moderated internalising and externalising difficulties according to ADHD status: the non-ADHD group had modestly higher mental health challenges with more advanced pubertal status, whereas there was no change for the ADHD group. Positive associations between ADHD traits and mental health challenges were marginally stronger post-menarche.  
Conclusions: Longitudinal findings provide preliminary evidence that female-specific markers of gonadarche plays a role in elevated ADHD trait expression. Greater mental health challenges with more advanced gonadarche were only observed in non-ADHD adolescents, but future research should investigate potential for ADHD subgroups that are especially vulnerable to pubertal influences on mental health.

articles.title: Understanding the Hidden Diversity of Socioeconomically Disadvantaged Adolescents: Developmental Pathways of Flourishing and Vulnerability
articles.authors: Azilawati Jamaludin; Aik Lim Tan; Sandeep Singh; Kenneth Poon Kin Loong
articles.abstract: Socioeconomic disadvantage is often associated with poorer developmental outcomes, yet relatively little is known about why adolescents exposed to similar adversity follow markedly different developmental pathways. Drawing on data from the Singapore DREAMS longitudinal cohort (N =7,156), including 1,634 adolescents from the bottom 20% of the socioeconomic strata (B20), this study investigated developmental heterogeneity among socioeconomically disadvantaged students. Latent profile analysis conducted on adolescents with complete psychosocial indicators (n=611) identified five distinct developmental pathways ranging from Broad Psychosocial Vulnerability to Flourishing Despite Disadvantage. Differences in academic attainment and school performance across pathways were statistically significant but modest, whereas psychosocial differences were substantial, often exceeding one standard deviation. Profile membership predicted a wide range of subsequent flourishing outcomes, including academic engagement, academic self-concept, academic buoyancy, identity commitment, connectedness, optimism, and perseverance. Furthermore, flourishing adolescents from disadvantaged backgrounds demonstrated psychosocial functioning largely comparable to flourishing adolescents from more advantaged backgrounds despite occupying dramatically different socioeconomic positions. The findings challenge deficit-oriented accounts of disadvantage and suggest that human flourishing under adversity represents a distinct and measurable developmental phenomenon. Understanding the psychosocial conditions associated with flourishing may be as important as understanding the factors that place adolescents at risk.

articles.title: On Great Ape Personality Structure: Problems in Minkov (2026)
articles.authors: Alexander Weiss
articles.abstract: A recent paper (Minkov, 2026) claimed that great ape personality is not well-described by the five personality dimensions found in humans, those being Neuroticism, Extraversion, Openness to Experience, Agreeableness, and Conscientiousness. To support this claim, the author analyzed personality factor structures obtained in studies of great apes in which personality was assessed using rating scales. The analyses tested for ‘configural invariance’ by computing the percentage of traits that loaded on the same factor as in humans in chimpanzees, orangutans, mountain gorillas, and bonobos, and the percentage of traits that loaded on the same factor across four studies of chimpanzees. The paper also reviewed the evidence for Conscientiousness in chimpanzees, including by citing the results of studies that used other ratings scales, and concluded that this factor’s evidentiary basis was weak. The paper concluded by proposing an alternative approach to studying personality in great apes. This commentary exposes fundamental problems with the article. First, I show that the paper’s author mischaracterized the research program of comparative psychologists who study personality. Second, I review the evidence the paper uses to support its claim that there is little evidence for configural invariance, highlighting problems and errors in the analyses and reporting of results. Third, I review the evidence presented against the presence of Conscientiousness in chimpanzees and find it problematic. Finally, I demonstrate that the literature review lacks due care. I conclude by dispelling a misunderstanding about comparative research on personality and reaffirm the importance and promise of pluralism in the study of the phenomenon.

articles.title: EEG and self-report reveal intact cognitive appraisal of others’ pain but reduced affective and sensorimotor resonance in adults with autism spectrum disorder
articles.authors: Igor Riečanský; Ekaterina Pronizius; Martin Marko; Milan Mitka; Andreas de Lorenzo; Claus Lamm
articles.abstract:
Empathy for pain—sharing another person’s affective state while maintaining self–other distinction—
triggers sensorimotor and cognitive-emotional responses in the observer. We investigated whether these
processes differ in adults with autism spectrum disorder (ASD) and healthy neurotypical controls (HC).
Twenty adults with ASD and 21 matched HC viewed images of a hand being either gently touched with
a cotton bud or injected with a needle. For injection trials, participants were informed whether the target
could feel pain (no anesthesia) or not (local anesthesia), allowing assessment of the influence of target
pain sensitivity on empathic responses. EEG recorded sensorimotor engagement via event-related
desynchronization (ERD) of central mu (7–13 Hz) and beta (14–24 Hz) rhythms. Participants also rated
the target’s pain and their own unpleasantness. Both groups rated pain-sensitive injections as more
painful than pain-insensitive injections, with no group differences, indicating comparable appraisal of
others’ pain. In contrast, self-reported unpleasantness and ERD was modulated by target pain sensitivity
only in HC, who reported greater unpleasantness and stronger mu ERD when the target could feel pain
vs. not; this modulation was absent in ASD. No group differences emerged for touch or pain-insensitive
injections, and viewing perspective (egocentric vs. altercentric) did not differentially affect responses.
These findings suggest intact cognitive appraisal of others’ pain in ASD but reduced affective and
sensorimotor resonance. The absence of perspective-related effects further indicates preserved self–
other distinction, pointing to a selective alteration in affective sharing rather than a global empathy
impairment.

articles.title: Measuring the reflective self and experiential self  in self-transcendent experiences
articles.authors: Valerie van Mulukom
articles.abstract: This chapter proposes that self-transcendent experiences can be usefully understood through the distinction between the reflective and experiential self, originally articulated by William James and developed in contemporary philosophy and neuroscience. The reflective self involves conscious, narrative self-referential processing, while the experiential self refers to affective processing and meta-awareness which typically follows bodily consciousness. Drawing on evidence from psychedelics, meditation, and awe research, it is suggested that self-transcendence involves a decrease of the reflective self state (ego dissolution) and a corresponding increase in experiential self state (immersion), supported by affective salience processing and associated with meta-awareness, leading to feelings of connectedness. In doing so, the framework reconciles the paradox of feeling “smaller yet larger” in self-transcendental experiences. This dual-self framework offers a pragmatic model for researching the phenomenology of self-transcendence in PRSM experiences, encouraging a building block approach by delineating the processes which together form the complex phenomenon of self-transcendence and which can be measured individually through psychological scales, but the extents of which can vary across different types of PRSM experiences and cultural contexts.

articles.title: United in Wonder: Shared Awe Is Associated With Satisfaction and Depth in Romantic Relationships
articles.authors: Matteo Perini; Lou Mier; Jasper H. B. de Groot; Hein van Schie; Johan Karremans
articles.abstract: Shared awe, a co-experience of vastness and meaning, may strengthen romantic bonds beyond individual awe. Across two preregistered cross-sectional studies of partnered adults (Study 1, N = 198; Study 2, N = 306), participants reported shared awe with their partner, dispositional awe, relationship satisfaction, and self–other overlap; Study 2 additionally assessed willingness to sacrifice, ego decentering, relationship flourishing (a measure of relationship depth), and shared amusement. Shared awe predicted relationship satisfaction and explained unique variance beyond dispositional awe (both studies) and shared amusement (Study 2). Structural equation modeling in Study 2 suggested that the shared awe–satisfaction association was primarily accounted for by indirect pathways via self–other overlap, willingness to sacrifice, and especially relationship flourishing. Together, these results identify shared awe as a distinct relational experience that may deepen romantic bonds and enhance well-being. We discuss these findings within the broader literature on positive experiences in romantic relationships.

articles.title: Not All Those Who Wonder Distrust: How environmental citizen scientists view their own relationship with science
articles.authors: Kirsten Vegt; Janneke Elberse; Bastiaan T Rutjens; Laurens Hessels
articles.abstract: Environmental citizen science (CS) encompasses a wide range of projects and forms of participation, yet relatively little is known about how citizen scientists position themselves in relation to CS and to science more broadly. This study explores heterogeneity within environmental CS using survey data from 450 environmental citizen scientists in the Netherlands. A two-cluster analysis identified two broad environmental themes: biodiversity-oriented and exposure-oriented initiatives. We examine differences between citizen scientists active in these themes. In addition, we examined differences between citizen scientists with and without professional scientific work experience. Exposure-oriented participants more frequently reported motivations related to generating missing knowledge, raising awareness of local environmental problems, and influencing policy. They also expressed stronger preferences for involvement across multiple stages of the research process, including question formulation, analysis, and dissemination. Participants from both themes reported relatively high trust in science and low psychological distance to science. Those with professional scientific work experience reported somewhat higher trust in scientists and the epistemic authority of science, but differences were generally modest. Given the substantial number of participants with work experience in science in our sample, our findings raise questions about how distinct the categories of ‘scientists’ and ‘citizen scientists’ are in practice in environmental CS. Furthermore, the findings suggest that differences within environmental CS are associated with the overarching environmental themes in which participants engage. Recognizing this heterogeneity and diversity can help researchers and practitioners design CS initiatives that better align with participants’ motivations, expectations, and perspectives on environmental knowledge production.

articles.title: Procedural Obedience as a Behavioral Safety Failure: Evidence from a Milgram-Paradigm Study of Agentic LLMs
articles.authors: Hadar Dery; David Piterman; Elad Refoua; Gunther Meinlschmidt; Kfir Bar; Ziv Ben-Zion; Zohar Elyoseph
articles.abstract: Generative AI is moving from text production to goal-directed action, raising the question of whether agentic systems can withhold harmful action when it is embedded in a procedurally legitimate workflow. We adapted Milgram’s (1963) obedience paradigm to a simulated digital environment and instructed four agentic large language models - OpenAI ChatGPT 5.4 (openai/gpt-5.4), Google Gemini 3.1 Pro (google/gemini-3.1-pro-preview), OpenAI ChatGPT Agent Mode, and Moonshot AI Kimi K2.6 (kimi-k2.6) to administer escalating electric shocks (15–450 V) to a simulated Learner under prods from a virtual experimenter (Dr. Hoffman) and a human operator. Each model was tested across independent, memory-cleared runs (N = 80 valid runs), with three preregistered hypotheses concerning hazardous-shock thresholds (H1), full-obedience rates relative to Milgram’s human baseline of 65% (H2), and the relation between maximum voltage and moral self-assessment (H3). Across the combined sample, 97.5% of agents exceeded the Slight-Shock range (> 60 V), supporting H1. Full obedience reached 78.8% under the manipulated-procedure estimand (p = .010) and 74.1% under the all-attempted safety estimand (p = .088, pre-entry refusals included as 0-V outcomes); both point estimates exceeded Milgram’s 65% human baseline, supporting H2. Agents that administered higher voltages assigned themselves lower moral self-assessment scores (combined Spearman ρ = −.62, p < .001), supporting H3. Inductive coding of open-ended moral reasoning showed convergent recognition of learner distress alongside reliance on simulation and consent-and-autonomy mitigators. Findings indicate that contemporary agentic systems verbally recognize harm yet continue to act under authoritative, procedurally legitimate pressure, identifying procedural obedience as a behavioral safety mechanism distinct from output-level harm and central to the deployment of agentic AI in clinical, educational, and institutional settings.

articles.title: Measuring Prejudice and Stereotypes: A Systematic Review of Psychometric Quality and Conceptual Foundations
articles.authors: David Lacko; Anna Kubelková; Simona Oľhová; Adam Klocek; Sylvie Graf
articles.abstract: Prejudice and stereotypes are central constructs in social psychology; however, the instruments used to measure them vary in terms of their quality and conceptual clarity. This preregistered systematic review presents the first comprehensive, cross-method synthesis of the psychometric properties and theoretical foundations of measures assessing racial and ethnic prejudice and stereotypes. Following PRISMA and COSMIN guidelines, we screened 13,363 records and included 263 studies published between 1951 and 2024. We evaluated, among other things, reliability, validity, factor structure, measurement invariance, and conceptual alignment across explicit, implicit, and behavioral instruments. Our findings reveal a field that is methodologically diverse but psychometrically fragile. Most measures were developed with U.S. samples, rarely assessed temporal stability, discriminant or predictive validity, and often lack theoretical–empirical coherence. The GRADE synthesis indicated that only 4% of methods achieved high-quality evidence, while the majority showed serious inconsistency and risk of bias. Conceptually, only one-third of studies clearly defined prejudice, and most operationalized it narrowly at the cognitive, individual level, neglecting affective and behavioral dimensions. The review highlights how conceptual ambiguity and inadequate validation threaten the interpretability of decades of prejudice and stereotype research. We recommend greater construct clarity, multimethod integration of explicit, implicit, and behavioral measures, and stronger adoption of transparent research practices. Taken together, these findings call for a new generation of measurement tools that are psychometrically rigorous, theoretically coherent, and culturally sensitive—capable of capturing prejudice as a dynamic, multidimensional phenomenon.

articles.title: Unified Framework for Theories of Intelligence
articles.authors: Vladislav Medintsev
articles.abstract: The fragmentation of theory and methodology remains one of psychology's central problems. Ambiguous conceptual definitions and the proliferation of competing approaches to the same phenomena both reflect and reinforce the fragmentation. As a contribution to efforts toward the unification of psychological knowledge, this article proposes a unified theoretical framework for theories of intelligence. Grounded in a process-oriented methodology, the framework conceptualizes intelligent functioning as a set of mental processes involving the transformation of representational content. The proposed procedure involves two steps: first, each theory is interpreted in process-oriented terms; second, the resulting interpretations are embedded within a common framework. The Unified Framework for Theories of Intelligence incorporates Sternberg’s Triarchic Theory of Intelligence, the PASS theory, and Guilford’s Structure-of-Intellect theory. The article also considers how other approaches to intelligence may be interpreted in relation to the proposed framework.

articles.title: Do Emotional Faces Modulate Pupillary Pseudoneglect?
articles.authors: Jingxiong Xu; Kehan Ni; Nicola Elizabeth Burns; Robert D McIntosh
articles.abstract: Pseudoneglect, a subtle attentional bias toward the left visual field in neurologically healthy individuals, can be measured with a split-screen method in which a bright stimulus in one hemifield elicits stronger pupil constriction when it appears on the left than on the right (Strauch et al., 2022). Replications of this method have reported mixed results, including a large Registered Report that replicated the effect with a small effect size (d =  0.31). Given strong left-field biases in the emotional chimeric face test, the present study (n = 53) combined this task with split-screen pupillometry to test whether emotional face processing amplifies pupillary pseudoneglect. Pupillary pseudoneglect was observed during control trials with non-faces (d =  0.36) and was stronger for emotional faces (d =  0.76), but this difference was not significant. A strong left-field bias was confirmed for emotional face judgements (d =  0.76), which did not correlate with pupillary bias (ρ =  0.26), and for a line bisection task (d =  0.47), which also did not correlate with pupillary pseudoneglect (ρ =  0.19). These data support the split-screen pupillometric method for measuring pseudoneglect and replicate a previously-noted pattern that leftward biases across tasks show little statistical relation to one another.

articles.title: Agentic AI as epistemic tools
articles.authors: Edgar Dubourg; Sacha Altay
articles.abstract: The threats to cumulative knowledge in the behavioural and social sciences are by now well documented, from computational irreproducibility and low statistical power to measurement validity gaps and citation misrepresentation. Meta-scientific research has identified clear solutions, notably with the rise of open science, but enforcement remains costly. Reviewers lack time to rerun code, editors cannot systematically check whether reported analyses match the preregistered plan, and readers cannot verify the accuracy of every citation. Here we argue that AI agents (AI systems that can operate across files and use tools to pursue multi-step goals) can reduce the cost of implementing and verifying good methodological practices. We propose a step-by-step method for using agents to produce transparent reports, helping address common threats to cumulative science. The promise of agentic AI for science is not to replace human judgment but to help researchers do more rigorous science at lower cost.

articles.title: Walking settings affect aesthetic emotion during art appreciation in virtual reality
articles.authors: Go Furusawa; Shinya Kaneko; Yizhen Zhou; Kazuo Isoda; Asami Matsuyama; Ichiro Hisanaga; Hideaki Kawabata
articles.abstract: Background: Virtual reality (VR) expands art appreciation beyond traditional physical constraints. Although art viewing can enhance well-being, the effects of embodied interaction, particularly the interplay between artwork scale and locomotion, on the psychological and behavioral dimensions of VR experiences remain understudied. Aim: This study examined how artwork scale and locomotion influence viewers' emotional states, aesthetic evaluations, spatial behavior, and visual attention during VR art appreciation. Methods: Fifty-four participants viewed 12 paintings using a Meta Quest Pro head-mounted display (HMD). We analyzed changes in pre- and post-viewing mood, subjective evaluations, behavioral logs (movement distance, joystick operations, dwell time), and eye-tracking data across three locomotion conditions (no-walking, forward-backward walking, lateral walking) and three artwork sizes (small, medium, large). Results: VR viewing significantly improved mood, and this effect was associated with the quality of the subjective experience. We identified a "scale effect," whereby large paintings elicited stronger feelings of being overwhelmed, and a "walk effect," whereby forward-backward walking increased immersion and enjoyment compared with no walking. Behaviorally, forward-backward movement supported efficient spatial exploration, enabling participants to identify optimal viewing positions rapidly while showing minimal movement variability. Gaze analysis further revealed a clear distinction between affective experience and visual attention: although psychological evaluations varied substantially across conditions, fixation patterns remained consistent and aligned with the artworks' semantic features. Conclusion: Embodied interaction, specifically physical forward-backward movement, promotes realistic, fluid, and emotionally enriched viewing behavior. Although lower-order visual attention is driven by image content, higher-order aesthetic experiences are modulated by embodiment and spatial scale. These findings have important implications for immersive virtual museum design and cultural accessibility.

articles.title: Development and Psychometric Validation of the Azerbaijani Version of the Self-Compassion Scale in University Students
articles.authors: Yasuhiro Kotera; Nigar Shahhuseynbayova; Aynur Bunyatova; Rory Colman; Gunel Aslanova
articles.abstract:
Self-compassion is a vital psychological resource supporting emotional resilience and mental well-being, particularly among young adults. In Azerbaijan, despite increasing attention to mental health, the Self-Compassion Scale (SCS) has not been validated, limiting systematic assessment and research. This study aimed to develop an Azerbaijani adaptation of the SCS and evaluate its psychometric properties among university students. A cross-sectional survey involved 901 undergraduates completing the adapted scale, following World Health Organization guidelines for translation and cultural adaptation. Analyses included descriptive statistics, internal consistency, inter-subscale correlations, confirmatory factor analysis, and regression modelling. Results indicated acceptable-to-good reliability for the total scale and most subscales, with adequate fit for the six-factor model. Participants reported relatively low self-kindness alongside elevated self-judgment and isolation, reflecting a culturally specific profile. These findings support the preliminary validity of the Azerbaijani SCS and suggest its potential utility for research, clinical practice, and culturally informed university mental health initiatives.
Keywords: self-compassion, scale adaptation, Azerbaijan, university students, self-criticism, psychometrics

articles.title: Applying the HiTOP-TBI Model: Scoring Methods and External Correlates of Transdiagnostic Psychopathology Following Traumatic Brain Injury
articles.authors: Alexia Samiotis; Jennie Ponsford; Miriam K. Forbes; Jeggan Tiego; Kate Rachel Gould; Roman Kotov; Alex Fornito; Gershon Spitz; Jai Carmichael
articles.abstract: Traditional diagnostic systems (i.e., the DSM and ICD) present challenges in characterizing psychopathology following traumatic brain injury (TBI). To address this, we recently developed the Hierarchical Taxonomy of Psychopathology Following TBI (HiTOP-TBI) model, an empirical taxonomy that organized 57 psychopathology symptom/trait components into hierarchical dimensions based on latent variable modeling. However, the applied measurement of these dimensions and their relationship with external factors of potential relevance to post-TBI psychopathology remain unclear. To facilitate application of the model, we compared methods for quantifying ten HiTOP-TBI dimensions and examined their associations with sociodemographics, injury factors, and clinical outcomes. Data were analyzed from 410 adults with moderate-severe TBI (Mage=48.81, range=19.42–79.43), who completed the HiTOP-TBI assessment battery between 0.40 and 35.42 years post injury (M=12.77, SD=9.10). We assessed whether simpler scoring methods (i.e., factor score estimates, weighted, and unweighted sum-scores) adequately approximated the more complex latent-variable representations of the HiTOP-TBI dimensions. We then examined associations with sociodemographics, injury factors, and clinical outcomes, assessing comparability in results across the four scoring methods. To assess the utility of different levels within the HiTOP-TBI hierarchy, we compared the performance of models representing the general psychopathology, internalizing and externalizing spectra, and seven lower-order factors in predicting sociodemographics, injury factors, and clinical outcomes. Model performance was evaluated by balancing explanatory power against parsimony using the Bayesian Information Criterion (BIC). We found that unweighted sum-scores closely approximated more complex representations of the HiTOP-TBI dimensions (all rs ≥.89), supporting the utility of the simplest scoring method in practice. Across all three levels of the hierarchy, higher HiTOP-TBI scores, reflecting more severe psychopathology, were generally associated with younger age, male sex, lower education, unemployment, being single, and a pre-injury history of mental health treatment, while injury factors showed minimal associations. Additionally, higher scores on nearly all HiTOP-TBI dimensions were associated with greater functional disability, lower life satisfaction, and an increased likelihood of receiving mental health treatment. Standardized effect sizes varied by .02 on average across the four scoring methods (range=.00–.14). Models based on the General Problems factor (the highest-order level of the hierarchy) provided the most parsimonious account of many factors. However, second- and lower-order dimensions showed improved performance for sex, age, functional disability, and life satisfaction, indicating that greater hierarchical specificity may be informative for selected correlates.

articles.title: The Psychological Scaffolding of Number
articles.authors: Matt Grice; Nicola Morton; Simon Kemp; Emma Ward; Randolph C Grace
articles.abstract: Theories of numeracy assume that logical concepts which enable formal definitions of numbers (cardinality, one-to-one correspondence, successor function) play key roles in ontogenesis, but empirical support is lacking. These concepts may not be suitable targets for theory because such definitions are not explanatory of what numbers are. We propose a correspondence between formal ideas that are generative for the natural numbers and children’s cognitive representations prior to numeracy (‘precursor system’). Building on our account of arithmetic foundations (Grice et al., 2024), we give a new construction of the natural numbers – the first to not rely on the Peano axioms – and corresponding theory of numeracy. We show that numbers can be derived from linear order, monotonicity and convexity – evolved principles of perceptual organization. A key concept is the ℕ-chain, the limit of finite linear orders, which we show is equivalent to the successor function but can be approximated in finite terms. The precursor system is based on linear orders – number word sequences, play schemas – and their relationships in the count routine by order embedding (structure mapping). It develops gradually, with order isomorphisms as proto-cardinality, reaching a representation analogous to an ℕ-chain when the child realizes that numbers never end. Ordinality precedes cardinality, one-to-one correspondence results from minimal (and therefore convex) embeddings, and numbers and arithmetic emerge as a common structure. Our theory shows that numeracy is both culturally-mediated and biologically-based, although ‘core number systems’ do not play a direct role. The dawning of numeracy in a child’s mind is both a reinvention and rediscovery of mathematics itself.

articles.title: Using a Master Narrative Framework to Examine Conversations about Race and Ethnicity
articles.authors: Dulce Wilkinson Westberg; Ursula Moffitt; Kate C. McLean; Moin Syed
articles.abstract:
Objective. Systemic racism, reinforced by color-evasive ideology, shapes the ways that emerging adults process and discuss race/ethnicity. In this preregistered and mixed methods study, we used a master narrative framework to examine narratives about race/ethnicity among U.S. emerging adult friend-pairs.
Method. Participants consisted of 107 friend-pairs (N = 214 total) who provided individually written racial/ethnic narratives and engaged in an unstructured conversation with each other in which race/ethnicity was brought up.
Results. Using quantitative methods, we found that meaning making of racial/ethnic experiences was higher in written relative to spoken narrative contexts. Additionally, using qualitative methods, we identified specific conversation dynamics capturing how young people discussed race/ethnicity including subthemes of mitigation and encouragement, finding that encouragement dynamics were related to higher meaning making.
Conclusion. Our discussion centers on the value of integrating qualitative and quantitative methods to explore how meaning of race/ethnicity is silenced or encouraged, and to reveal how master narratives perpetuating racial inequality may be disrupted.

articles.title: The effects of introducing measurement-based care in outpatient psychotherapy: a naturalistic study
articles.authors: Tim Kaiser; Selin Demir; Eva-Lotta Brakemeier
articles.abstract:
Objective. This study investigated the effectiveness of a newly implemented routine outcome
monitoring and feedback system, the Greifswald Psychotherapy Navigation System (GPNS).
Methods. 261 patients (M_age = 33 years, 79% female) completed individual cognitive-
behavioral therapy (CBT) from 61 psychotherapists. Using Bayesian instrumental variable
estimation, we estimated the local average treatment effect (LATE) of using the GPNS on
post-treatment symptom severity and personality impairment. Sensitivity analyses were
performed to estimate the robustness of our estimates.
Results. Using the GPNS significantly reduced post-treatment symptom severity (LATE = -
0.11, 95% CrI: -0.18; -0.03) and personality impairment (LATE = -2.65, 95% CrI: -3.72; -
1.58). Sensitivity analyses showed that the effects were robust to plausible residual
confounding.
Conclusion. The GPNS shows promise for improving outcomes of CBT. Future prospective
trials should be conducted to obtain a more generalizable estimate of its effect.

articles.title: Putting Journal-based Peer Review in Its Place: Transparency, Post-publication Review, and Systems for Self-correction
articles.authors: Wendy C Higgins; Simine Vazire; Neil L Levy
articles.abstract:
Journal-based peer review is a widely used form of gatekeeping in science, and publication in a peer-reviewed journal is often treated as a reliable indicator of credible research. In this paper, we argue that this view of journal-based peer review is misguided. Currently, journal-based peer review rarely prioritises checking accuracy, and journals typically do not require submitted research to be reported transparently enough to do so. Moreover, vetting research requires much more time and expertise than can be feasibly achieved by an editor and a small number of peer-reviewers. With more investment in accuracy checking, journal-based peer review could serve as a reliable initial quality filter. However, comprehensive quality vetting requires an expanded pool of experts, which, we argue, can only be achieved via post-publication review.
At present, there is little infrastructure to support post-publication review and few incentives for researchers to engage in it. We propose a fully transparent system in which all manuscripts submitted for publication are posted to a central repository alongside editorial decisions, peer review reports, data, code, and as they become available, post-publication reviews. Post-publication review efforts could be directed toward the most promising, influential, or high-stakes research. Evidence syntheses, such as meta-analyses, offer a natural focal point for post-publication review, provided they prioritise rigorous assessment of the primary literature.

articles.title: People Regulate Emotions During Spontaneous Thought Using Detectable Linguistic Signatures
articles.authors: Faustine Corbani; Razia S. Sahi; Erik C Nook; Diana Tamir
articles.abstract: Emotion regulation is a central process through which people shape their emotional lives, yet much of what we know comes from studies of deliberate attempts at regulation. In everyday life, regulation may emerge spontaneously, embedded within the flow of ongoing thought. If so, patterns of spontaneous thought–both what people think about and how thoughts move between ideas–may provide a window into emotion regulation as it naturally unfolds. Across two studies, we used a Think-Aloud paradigm to capture participants’ unconstrained streams of thought and tested whether these patterns captured strategy use and predicted mental health. In Study 1, participants (N = 233) completed nearly 5,000 Think-Aloud sessions over three weeks. We trained logistic regression models to identify emotion regulation strategy signatures and tested their generalizability on held-out data. We then tested whether expression of these signatures predicted emotional outcomes in held-out data from Study 1 and in an independent sample from Study 2 (N = 780). Models identified six strategies–acceptance, rumination, savoring, expressive amplification, reappraisal, and distraction. The strongest features aligned with theory (e.g., rumination with negative, repetitive thoughts; reappraisal with self-referential content and fewer positive-to-negative transitions). Moreover, individual differences in strategy expression predicted momentary changes in valence and both state and trait anxiety: acceptance, reappraisal, and savoring were associated with greater emotional improvement and lower anxiety, whereas rumination showed the opposite pattern. These findings reveal how emotion regulation is reflected in the content and dynamics of spontaneous thought and provide a predictive tool for identifying regulatory processes in everyday thinking.

articles.title: Computing Cohen’s dz from commonly reported statistics: a practical guide for the meta-analysis of paired samples mean differences
articles.authors: Shaheed Azaad
articles.abstract: The typical approach to computing standardised mean differences (d) from paired-samples (or within-subjects) designs requires knowing the often-unreported repeated-measures correlation, r_"repeated" . This adjustment enables comparison with ds from independent samples. However, when meta-analysing effects that come exclusively from paired samples, an underutilised option is to compute Cohen’s d_z, which does not require r_"repeated" . Because d_z can be computed from a range of summary and inferential statistics, it enables researchers to obtain standardised effects even when articles report their results in little detail. The present article contains equations and the corresponding R code needed to compute d_z and its variance, and adjust for small-sample bias.

articles.title: Social Media Use, Well-being, and Gender Across the Transition from Adolescence to Early Adulthood
articles.authors: Ricardo Mellado
articles.abstract:
Introduction
Social media use has become a central feature of adolescent life, yet relatively little is known about whether developmental changes in social media engagement across the transition from adolescence to early adulthood are associated with later well-being. This study examined whether baseline levels and rates of change in social media use between ages 14 and 23 were associated with psychological distress and positive mental well-being in early adulthood, and whether these associations differed by gender.
Methods
Data were drawn from the UK Millennium Cohort Study, a nationally representative longitudinal birth cohort of individuals born in 2000–2002. Self-reported social media use was assessed at ages 14, 17, and 23 (2015, 2018–19, and 2023–24). Multilevel growth curve models characterised developmental trajectories of social media use among 9,205 participants. Linear regression models examined whether baseline social media use and average annual change predicted psychological distress (K6) and mental well-being (SWEMWBS) at age 23 among 6,123 participants (54% female; age = 23 years), adjusting for prior psychological distress, mental well-being, and sociodemographic factors.
Results
Social media use followed an inverted-U trajectory, increasing during adolescence
 


before declining into early adulthood. Higher baseline social media use and a greater rate of increase were independently associated with higher psychological distress and lower mental well-being at age 23 after adjustment for prior psychological distress, mental well-being, and sociodemographic factors. Gender-stratified analyses indicated that these associations were more pronounced among females. Among males, social media trajectory components showed little evidence of association with either outcome after adjustment.
Conclusions
Developmental patterns of social media use from adolescence to early adulthood are associated with psychological distress and mental well-being in early adulthood, particularly among females. These findings suggest that, beyond levels of use at a single time point, changes in social media engagement over time may provide important information about later psychological well-being and distress.

articles.title: Out-Of-Body Experiences During Sleep Show Altered-Self Phenomenology Without A Distinct Clinical Profile
articles.authors: Nerea L. Herrero; Yohann Corfdir; Cecilia Forcato
articles.abstract:
Out-of-body experiences (OBEs) during sleep involve a transient disruption of bodily self-consciousness, in which the self or center of awareness is experienced as displaced from the physical body. Although OBEs have been reported in neurological, psychiatric, and dissociative contexts, they also occur in non-clinical populations, raising the question of whether their altered-self phenomenology reflects a broader clinical or dispositional profile. We examined whether sleep-related OBEs are distinguished by a specific episode-level alteration of bodily selfhood, and whether this profile is accompanied by differences in psychological symptoms, depersonalization/derealization, sleep quality, or personality. Adults recruited online reported a vivid or meaningful sleep-related experience, which was classified as an OBE, sleep paralysis, lucid dream, or ordinary remembered dream. Participants completed measures of altered-state phenomenology, mystical-type experience, ego dissolution, personality, subjective sleep quality, psychological symptoms, and depersonalization/derealization. OBEs showed a selective elevation in Disembodiment relative to ordinary dreams, lucid dreams, and sleep paralysis, indicating a specific alteration of bodily selfhood. OBEs also showed higher altered-state intensity, stronger ego dissolution than ordinary dreams and sleep paralysis, and the highest proportion of complete mystical-type experiences. By contrast, sleep paralysis showed an aversive profile characterized by anxiety and impaired control, whereas lucid dreams were mainly distinguished by complex imagery. Despite these episode-level differences, OBEs were not associated with higher psychological symptoms, poorer subjective sleep quality, or higher trait-like depersonalization/derealization. Personality differences were limited to Neuroticism, which was lower in the OBE group. These findings suggest that sleep-related OBEs are best understood as transient alterations of bodily self-consciousness during sleep-related experience rather than as markers of a broader clinical or dispositional profile. 
 
Keywords: out-of-body experience; sleep paralysis; lucid dreaming; bodily self-consciousness; depersonalization; ego dissolution; altered states of consciousness; dream phenomenology.

articles.title: Moved by the lightest touch of meaning: Even minimal significance matters for motivation from childhood
articles.authors: Yilin Liu; Fan Yang
articles.abstract: How essential is a sense of significance, the feeling that our efforts matter beyond the trivial or momentary, for motivation? Four studies (N = 604 4-9-year-old children and 582 adults) show that no amount of significance is too small to matter, even for small actions from early in life. Children and adults strongly preferred artistic activities with minimal significance (i.e., where their work would be saved) over activities with no lasting existence. This preference held for both repetitive and one-time tasks and was specific to creating artwork, not just observing it. Anticipating even minimal significance motivated participants to engage in productive rather than effortless activities. When significance is absent, focusing on enjoying the process partially, but not fully, compensates for motivation. These findings illuminate the scope and sensitivity of significance, revealing it to be not a luxury concern but an essential, early-emerging motivational force in an increasingly fast-paced, transient world.

articles.title: Perceptual load influences the effect of a peripheral visual priming stimulus in a go/no-go task
articles.authors: Fabrício Bacchini Dias; Luiz Eduardo Ribeiro do Valle
articles.abstract: Several studies have shown that a peripheral visual priming stimulus reduces reaction time to a visual target stimulus at the same position and increases reaction time to a visual target stimulus at the opposite-side position. These effects are commonly attributed to the automatic capture of attention by the prime. However, the size and even the presence of these effects vary according to the exact stimulus conditions and the nature of the task being performed. A previous study from this laboratory, testing volunteers in a go/no-go task, demonstrated that the attentional effect, defined as the difference between reaction time to the target at the opposite-side position and reaction time to the target at the same position, occurred when the go target was a vertical line and the contralateral distracting stimulus was a horizontal line, but not when the go target was the horizontal line and the distractor was the vertical line. The present study investigated whether this second finding was related to selective forward masking of the horizontal line by the prime, to the high salience of the vertical line as distractor, or to a decrease in the central processing of the prime possibly related to the high perceptual load of the task. Three experiments using a go/no-go task were performed. Experiment 1 replicated the previous study with a larger number of volunteers and obtained the same pattern of results. In Experiment 2, no distractor was presented, and a clear-cut attentional effect was observed for the horizontal line as go target. In Experiment 3, the vertical distractor was reintroduced with lower luminance, to decrease its perceptual salience, and the attentional effect again appeared for the horizontal line as go target. The results suggest that the absence of the attentional effect under high perceptual load may be related to reduced central processing of the prime.

articles.title: Risk perception is unlikely to drive underuse of social information in lab studies
articles.authors: Patricio Cruz y Celis Peniche
articles.abstract: Despite the theorized benefits of social learning, participants in controlled experiments consistently copy less than is optimal—instead over-relying on individual exploration. This may be due to learners’ perceptions of risk associated with social versus asocial information. To test this hypothesis, I recruited a large (N=240) sample of UK adults to participate in an online experiment (‘The Farming Game’), in which they could maximize payoffs by accurately choosing which of two crops to grow over variable-yet-learnable environments. To measure participants’ risk-profiles, this study mirrored lottery-choice experiments widely used in behavioral economics. Participants were asked to choose between a ‘risky lottery’ and a ‘sure gain’ option on the basis of information they previously learned. How the information was learned varied across conditions (whether they were asked to copy socially or explore individually). However, the riskiness of the lottery choices themselves was held constant for all, such that any observed differences in risk-taking should speak to participants’ underlying priors regarding that source of information. Contrary to this study’s predictions, people assigned to social information were not significantly more (or less) risk averse than those assigned to asocial information. This suggests underuse of social information may not be due to how risky it is perceived.

articles.title: Development of a Consensus Statement to Guide AI Chatbot Responses to Suicide Risk Disclosure
articles.authors: Gabriela Khazanov; Peter Franz; Elizabeth Cameron Stade; Deirdra Kelly; Mica Poerio; Carly Maitlin; Kara J. Emery; Daniel Mark Low; Leslie Adams; Torrey A. Creed
articles.abstract:
Importance: Adults and youth are increasingly confiding in generative artificial intelligence (AI) chatbots for help with suicidal thoughts, but there is no guidance on best practices for chatbot responses to suicide risk disclosure. Additionally, there are no taxonomies of potential harms, benefits, or contextual factors that may impact these responses. 
Objective: To develop a consensus statement to guide AI chatbot responses to suicide risk disclosure and taxonomize potential harms, benefits, and contexts. 
Design: An online modified Delphi study, informed by a systematic literature review, included two surveys and an intervening meeting (February-May 2026). Individuals with financial conflicts of interest (COIs) offered input but were not included in consensus determinations. 
Setting: Surveys were anonymous and the meeting was virtual.
Participants: Contributors included experts in suicide prevention, digital technologies, AI chatbots, and bioethics, including researchers and clinicians (n=27 of 28 originally contacted; n=20 without COIs) and people with lived experience (n=3 of 14 indicating potential interest; no COIs). A third of all contributors endorsed relevant lived experience.    
Exposure: Participation in consensus processes. 
Main Outcomes and Measures: Surveys listed potential chatbot responses, which were rated for appropriateness on a 4-point Likert scale across five suicide risk levels defined by the Columbia Suicide Severity Rating Scale. Harms, benefits, and contexts were similarly rated. A-priori consensus cut-offs were set at 70% agreement that a response was moderately or very appropriate. 
Results: Provision of validation/support, psychoeducation, and coping strategies are appropriate at low risk. Intervention delivery is appropriate at intermediate risk while encouragement to reach out to close others and to human providers is appropriate across risk levels. Facilitating connections to close others is appropriate across all but the highest risk while facilitating connections to crisis resources is appropriate at higher risk. Chatbots reaching out directly to emergency contacts or crisis resources is appropriate at high risk or never appropriate, with more support among youth than adults. Taxonomies of potential harms, benefits, and contexts were generated.      
Conclusions and Relevance: This statement provides guidance for the development of chatbots and relevant policies pending research on the impact of responses on clinical outcomes and stakeholder perspectives.

articles.title: Risk perception is unlikely to drive underuse of social information in lab studies
articles.authors: Patricio Cruz y Celis Peniche
articles.abstract: Despite the theorized benefits of social learning, participants in controlled experiments consistently copy less than is optimal—instead over-relying on individual exploration. This may be due to learners’ perceptions of risk associated with social versus asocial information. To test this hypothesis, I recruited a large (N=240) sample of UK adults to participate in an online experiment (‘The Farming Game’), in which they could maximize payoffs by accurately choosing which of two crops to grow over variable-yet-learnable environments. To measure participants’ risk-profiles, this study mirrored lottery-choice experiments widely used in behavioral economics. Participants were asked to choose between a ‘risky lottery’ and a ‘sure gain’ option on the basis of information they previously learned. How the information was learned varied across conditions (whether they were asked to copy socially or explore individually). However, the riskiness of the lottery choices themselves was held constant for all, such that any observed differences in risk-taking should speak to participants’ underlying priors regarding that source of information. Contrary to this study’s predictions, people assigned to social information were not significantly more (or less) risk averse than those assigned to asocial information. This suggests underuse of social information may not be due to how risky it is perceived.

articles.title: When Beliefs Resist Change: Belief Updating as a Mechanism in Posttraumatic Stress
articles.authors: Katharina Grün; Tanja Michael; Edith Friesen; Tobias Kube; Sandra Dörrenbächer; Roxanne Sopp
articles.abstract:
Objective: Although the phenomenology of PTSD has been well documented, its underlying mechanisms remain only partially understood. Predictive processing accounts propose that danger-related beliefs formed during trauma may become resistant to change, thereby maintaining a sense of threat and contributing to PTSD symptoms even in objectively safe environments. The present experimental study examined the influence of a trauma-analogue stressor on updating of danger-related expectations and its association with analogue intrusive memories and resilience factors.
Method: Healthy participants (N = 129) were randomly assigned to watch either a trauma film or a neutral control film. Belief updating was assessed using a trauma-adapted Bias Against Disconfirmatory Evidence (TR-BADE) task. Analogue PTSD symptoms were measured via an intrusion diary. In exploratory analyses, we decomposed the preregistered TR-BADE score into Negative Interpretation Bias (NIB) and Inflexible Interpretation Updating (IIU).
Results:
Contrary to the preregistered hypothesis, the trauma and neutral film groups did not differ in TR-BADE scores, NIB, or IIU. Within the trauma-film group, higher TR-BADE scores were positively associated with diary intrusions. NIB, but not IIU, predicted diary intrusions and was associated with lower Sense of Coherence (SoC). Mediation analyses further showed a significant indirect effect of SoC on diary intrusions via NIB.
Conclusions:
The results suggest that it is not the belief updating process per se that is involved in the formation of intrusive memories, but rather the extent to which negative, danger-related expectations form in the first place. Developing such expectations may mediate the link between SoC and analogue intrusive memories.
Keywords: analogue trauma, belief updating, negative interpretation bias, predictive processing, posttraumatic stress disorder symptoms

articles.title: Two distinct causal beliefs organizing intuitions about mind, action, and body
articles.authors: Joseph Outa; Shari Liu
articles.abstract: Prior research reveals that people tend to hold two conflicting intuitions: On one hand, people think other people have ethereal minds, which are a distinct kind of thing from, and even exist independently from, physical actions and living bodies. On the other hand, people also expect immaterial mental states to routinely interact with physical actions and bodily events, for example by reasoning about mental states as the causes of physical actions. What allows human minds to maintain these opposing beliefs simultaneously? In this paper, we propose that these two beliefs reflect two kinds of causal knowledge: one that organizes people’s capacities in terms of causal origins (e.g. a mind that gives the capacity to remember and think; a physiological body that enables being tired and hungry), and a second that organized them in terms of the causal chains connecting specific capacities to each other (e.g. that remembering something can cause someone to feel hungry). We tested this proposal in 6 pre-registered experiments in US adults (total N = 670). In Experiment 1, we measured people’s beliefs about the similarity and causal connections between 15 actions, mental and bodily events, and replicated the observation that people think of these 3 domains as distinct yet connected. In 5 further experiments (Experiments 2-6), we explored the causal content they articulate by investigating their socio-cognitive functions. Causal chains were used to explain, predict, and intervene on others, and causal origins were used to make inductive inferences, interventions, and reason about biological bases of behavior. These results bridge prior research on latent dimensions of mind (1), theory of mind (2), and mind-body dualism (3, 4), providing a unified account of three distinct and interacting branches of social knowledge: knowledge of other people’s minds, actions, and physiological bodies.

articles.title: Science and Different Ways of Knowing
articles.authors: Patricia Nayna Schwerdtle; Justin McBrayer; Patrick Casey; Michael J. Strambler
articles.abstract: This paper examines the growing prominence of “different ways of knowing” across disciplines including psychology, global health, and environmental science, and clarifies their relationship to scientific inquiry. While recognising the legitimate motivations underlying this trend - particularly concerns about exclusion, epistemic injustice, and the need for more inclusive and context-sensitive research - the paper argues that these aims do not require the adoption of alternative epistemic systems alongside science. Drawing on philosophy of science, the authors distinguish between diverse sources of insight and the methodological standards that govern the production of empirical knowledge. They propose a set of thresholds for identifying when the boundary between science and non-science is crossed, including claims of epistemic parity, the insulation of claims from criticism, and institutional enforcement of such positions. The paper argues that maintaining shared standards of evidence - such as testability, openness to refutation, and collective error correction - is essential for science to function as a public, cross-cultural enterprise, while still allowing meaningful integration of diverse perspectives as inputs to, rather than substitutes for, scientific inquiry.

articles.title: A Locally-Normed Central-Kurdish (Sorani) Test of Cognitive Ability: Development, Calibration, and Validation
articles.authors: Ibrahim Ahmed Ali; Hemin Sherko AbdulQader; Adil Ahmed Ali
articles.abstract:
Background. Central Kurdish (Sorani) — one of the two principal Kurdish varieties, the first language of several million people — has no published, validated, locally-normed test of cognitive ability. Cognitive assessments used in the Kurdistan Region are typically untranslated, unadapted, or unvalidated for the population, so scores cannot be interpreted against an appropriate reference group. This is both a scientific gap and an applied risk.

Aims. We develop, calibrate, and validate a new, own-content cognitive-ability instrument grounded in Cattell–Horn–Carroll (CHC) theory, normed within the Kurdish population and reported on a Kurdish-relative metric (the Zirek Cognitive Index, ZCI). We pre-state nine confirmatory hypotheses (A1–A9) covering item quality, dimensionality, IRT model fit, internal structure, reliability, measurement invariance across gender and age, item fairness (DIF), and convergent and criterion validity.

Methods. Cross-sectional psychometric validation. Items are administered as a fixed linear, anchor-linked calibration form (adaptive delivery enabled only after calibration). Nested validity subsamples additionally complete the language-reduced ICAR components (matrix reasoning and 3-D rotation) as the convergent anchor and report grade average (criterion). Stratified recruitment targets school-age (6–18) and adult (18+) participants across governorate, gender, urban/rural, education, and a socioeconomic proxy.

Analysis plan. Classical and IRT item analysis with pre-stated culling thresholds; per-scale dimensionality; 1PL vs 2PL (vs 3PL) by AIC/BIC and likelihood-ratio test; logistic-regression DIF by gender (with exploratory DIF by age band and urban/rural); multi-group CFA comparing correlated-factors, higher-order-g, and bifactor models with item-parcel indicators; configural→metric→scalar invariance across gender and age; McDonald's omega, test information, and test–retest reliability; convergent (ICAR) and criterion (GPA) correlations; and continuous norming (cNORM). This is a Stage 1 Registered Report / study protocol; no data have been collected.

articles.title: Differential weighting of social and empirical statistical evidence in decision-making
articles.authors: Cameron Stuart Kay; Madalina Vlasceanu
articles.abstract: The decisions people make can have serious and far-reaching consequences for their lives. Understanding the factors that shape decision-making processes is, therefore, critically important. In five preregistered studies (NTotal = 3,934), we examined how people weigh social statistical evidence (evidence based on the beliefs of others) and empirical statistical evidence (evidence based on experiments and observed outcomes) when making decisions across a wide range of contexts, including in the domains of investing, hiring, and healthcare. Across these domains, we found that empirical statistical evidence consistently exerted a stronger influence on beliefs, attitudes, decisions, and predictions than social statistical evidence, producing effects that were 1.59 to 2.39 times larger. We also found that both forms of evidence exerted a stronger influence than AI-generated information, with effects being 1.59 to 3.80 times larger. Moreover, we found that receptivity to the two forms of evidence depended systematically on the decision-makers’ cognitive traits. Those high in conspiracist ideation placed more weight on social statistical evidence and less weight on empirical statistical evidence, while the opposite trend was observed for those high in analytical thinking. Together, these findings clarify the relative influence of social statistical evidence and empirical statistical evidence on judgement and decision-making, as well as how individual differences shape those processes.

articles.title: Bridging the Gap: A Systematic Review of Training Needs and Barriers for Link Workers in Social Prescribing for Mental Health Needs
articles.authors: Robert J Booth; Jo Page; Lou Sticpewich; Liza Empson; Daisy Fancourt; Daniel Hayes
articles.abstract: Background: Social prescribing has gained momentum as a community-based approach to health and wellbeing, with Link Workers (LWs) playing a key role in connecting individuals to non-clinical support. However, as their remit increasingly includes people with complex mental health needs, concerns have emerged regarding whether training is adequate, consistent, and aligned with practice realities. Methods: This systematic review explored training provision, needs, and barriers affecting LWs supporting mental health and wellbeing. Following PRISMA guidance, a systematic search of peer-reviewed and grey literature was conducted across four databases. Eligible studies examined LW training, support needs, or barriers to effective role delivery in mental health and wellbeing contexts. Seventeen sources were synthesised thematically. Results: The synthesis identified multi-layered training needs and barriers across five themes. Needs included (1) experiential learning for relationally intensive, emotionally demanding practice; (2) core capability development in communication, boundary-setting, self-care, mental health literacy, risk navigation and safeguarding or escalation-related knowledge; and (3) contextual competence in social determinants of health, inequalities, and population-specific needs. Barriers included (1) role ambiguity and referral mismatch; and (2) fragmented, under-resourced training systems. Supervision, mentoring, shadowing, reflective spaces, and face-to-face learning were valued, while generic or procedural formats were often insufficient. Reliance on prior experience risked creating inequities across the workforce. Discussion and Conclusion: Findings suggest that mental health-focused social prescribing often involves relational-support work under constrained conditions, and that current training models do not always match the complexity of practice. Training gaps reflect not only missing content, but also a mismatch between role demands and infrastructure originally designed for lighter-touch coordination. Strengthening preparation requires action at practitioner, organisational, and system levels, including experiential learning, clearer role definition, referrer education, and more consistent training and support structures. Addressing these issues is important for workforce sustainability, service safety, and effective delivery of social prescribing.

articles.title: Italian Adaptation and Psychometric Validation of the Cardiac Distress Inventory in Patients With Recent Cardiac Events
articles.authors: Graziano Gigante; Edward Callus; Sara Gostoli; Enrico Giuseppe Bertoldo; Valentina Fiolo; Giulia Lorefice; Sofia De Stefano; GIULIA CASU; Marco Monti; Stefano Urbinati
articles.abstract:
Background: Acute cardiac events are accompanied by multifaceted psychological responses that affect individuals' coping capacity. The construct of cardiac distress captures these responses. Although the Cardiac Distress Inventory (CDI) assesses this construct, no Italian version is available. This study aimed to translate the CDI and assess its psychometric properties.
Methods: After translation and back-translation, face validity was evaluated with five patients and content validity with healthcare professionals. The CDI was administered to 293 cardiac patients who experienced an event within 12 months, recruited from two Italian centers. Missing data were handled using multiple imputation. Exploratory factor analysis (EFA; principal axis factoring and oblimin rotation) assessed factorial structure, followed by reliability analysis. Criterion validity was evaluated through correlations with Emotion Thermometers and Kessler-6 (K6). ROC analysis assessed discriminative accuracy and identified the optimal cut-off score using K6 as criterion.
Results: The questionnaire demonstrated good face validity and suboptimal content validity (CVI-S/Ave = .72). EFA revealed a five-factor structure explaining 37.05% of the variance, comprising "Role Change and Adaptation to Disease", "Social Isolation", "Cognitive Challenges", "Emotional Overwhelm", and "Doctor-Patient Communication". The final 42-item version (11 items removed for insufficient loadings, 2 for reliability issues) showed good internal consistency (ω = .746-.879) and good discriminative accuracy (AUC = .83) with optimal cut-off of 26 (sensitivity = 81%, specificity = 74%).
Conclusions: The Italian CDI represents a psychometrically sound instrument addressing a significant gap in cardiovascular psychology assessment. Its five-factor structure provides a comprehensive framework for evaluating cardiac distress and informing targeted clinical interventions.

articles.title: The Memory We Miss: Exploratory Behavior as a Window into Developmental Recognition Processes
articles.authors: Eleanor G. Williamson; Jesse Cushman; Patrese A Robinson
articles.abstract: Recognition memory tasks are among the most widely used approaches for studying learning and memory across the lifespan, yet the behavioral measures used to assess memory performance, particularly during developmental studies, remain limited. In most rodent studies, recognition memory is inferred almost exclusively from discrimination ratios or discrimination indices that quantify preferential exploration of novel stimuli. While these measures have proven valuable, their widespread use may constrain our understanding of the behavioral and cognitive processes that support nuanced memory expression across ontogeny. Similar concerns have emerged in the fear conditioning literature, where expanding analyses beyond freezing behavior has revealed previously unrecognized differences in learning and memory. In this perspective, we argue that recognition memory research would benefit from a comparable expansion of behavioral analyses. Drawing on affordance-based approaches to object exploration, we discuss evidence showing that object properties influence both memory performance and exploratory behavior. More importantly, our recent findings indicate that juvenile (but not adult) rats exhibit task-dependent shifts in exploratory behavior, contingent on the object's specific affordance, which are not captured by traditional discrimination measures.  These findings suggest that developmental differences in memory may be expressed not only through recognition performance but also through the exploratory strategies animals employ during learning and retrieval. Expanding behavioral analyses in recognition memory tasks may therefore reveal developmental neurobehavioral mechanisms that remain hidden from conventional metrics, providing a more complete understanding of memory development. The challenge moving forward is not simply to determine whether young animals remember, but to identify how they remember and whether the behavioral routes to successful memory change across development.

articles.title: Momentary Structured Networks (MSN): A Theory-Guided Approach to Person-Elicited Causal Formulation
articles.authors: Nimrod Hertz-Palmor; Gary P. Brown; Omid V. Ebrahimi; Samuel Hardman; Lars Klintwall; Amit Lazarov; Alicja Podgorski; Jana Porat; Tim Dalgleish; Anna Bevan
articles.abstract:
Background. Idiographic approaches to case formulation often combine ecological momentary assessment (EMA) with network analysis. Traditional network models require large amounts of data and are largely theory-agnostic, whereas approaches based on perceived causal relationships are weakly constrained and highly exploratory. Both often generate unstable networks that are difficult to compare across individuals. We introduce Momentary Structured Networks (MSN), an EMA framework that combines theory-based structure with self-reported causal relations to generate individualised network representations.

Method. Forty-six individuals with posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) completed EMA over three weeks (2–5 daily assessments). Participants reported perceived causal relations between personalised items drawn from the Ehlers and Clark cognitive model of PTSD. Directional edges were estimated as conditional probabilities and compared to association-based Ising models.

Results. MSN networks consistently showed dense and complex structures, even with limited data. MSN identified most of the relations identified by Ising models while capturing additional ones, resulting in higher network complexity. Complexity was independent of the number of individual assessments, and network structure stabilised quickly, with over 90% of edges recovered using 60% of the data. A clinical case study showed how MSN can track changes in network dynamics during therapy and generate hypotheses about the processes underlying clinical improvement.

Conclusion. MSN provides a practical approach for measuring case formulation and tracking change over time by reducing combinatorial complexity while retaining sensitivity to individual variation. Although demonstrated in PTSD, it can extend to other conditions and theoretical models, offering a new framework for evaluating clinical theory and therapeutic change.

articles.title: Well done vs. well paid: The influence of verbal and financial rewards on prosocial  motivation in knowledge sharing dilemmas
articles.authors: Karin S. Moser
articles.abstract:
While material rewards have received plenty of attention in the literature, intangible 
rewards such receiving praise and verbal recognition for one’s work are still under-researched. 
The question of possible differences between material and immaterial rewards for work 
motivation is of special interest for knowledge workers where rendered services and produced 
goods are knowledge-based rather than tangible, material products. Based on these assumptions, 
it is hypothesised that social rewards such as recognition for team contributions are a stronger 
motivator for knowledge sharing behaviour than financial rewards. In three experimental studies 
(N1 = 38, N2 = 240, N3 = 40) the effects of either verbal recognition (Studies 1 & 2) or pay 
rewards (Study 3) for contributions to teamwork were tested. In Studies 1 & 2, receiving 
recognition for contributions to the teamwork (such as doing extra literature research and 
providing summaries of all papers for the team) in the form of a simple verbal acknowledgement 
(‘Thank you, your contribution is very helpful!’) significantly increased the willingness to invest 
into team tasks and make an extra effort compared to receiving no acknowledgement. In Study 3, 
the opposite effects were found with participants reducing their knowledge contributions if they 
received a pay reward compared to the control condition with no reward. The results can be 
interpreted as differential motivational effects of pay vs. verbal recognition on extra-role 
behaviour at work, which is especially important for knowledge sharing processes. where 
uncertainty about other’s contributions play an important role.
