Exploratory analyses suggest perceived respect for hard work and deservingness of beneficiaries were associated with the support gap. Together, these results suggest Americans prefer policies that reduce inequality by influencing earnings more than through taxes and transfers.

articles.title: “Safe for now?” Boundary work and tensions of AI Adoption in Sign Language Interpreting and Speech-to-Text Reporting in Belgium and the Netherlands
articles.authors: Maartje De Meulder; Karolien Gebruers; Deborah Giustini
articles.abstract: This article examines how sign language interpreters (SLIs) and speech-to-text reporters (STTRs) in Belgium (Flanders) and the Netherlands understand and navigate artificial intelligence (AI)-driven language technologies in their professional lives. Drawing on an online survey (N = 134) conducted in 2025, and theorized through  boundary work, we identify three tensions structuring practitioners' accounts: a temporality tension, in which anticipated disruption coexists with present stability; an access tension, in which AI expands available options while enabling institutions to narrow the definition of "sufficient access"; and a professional identity tension, in which the technology threatening practitioners' livelihoods may simultaneously advance the profession's own emancipatory ideal. We argue that AI in language access is actively produced through practitioners’ ongoing boundary work and calls for recognizing them — alongside deaf consumers — as epistemic actors in governing just technological transitions.

articles.title: Where DESO Disappears: Spatial Inequality and Social Stratification at Labour Market Entry
articles.authors: Katy Morris
articles.abstract: At country-level, a host of evidence suggests there is a sizeable direct effect of social origin (DESO) on initial labour market outcomes, net of educational attainment. What is true at country-level is not always true below country-level, however. Using data from the UK Household Longitudinal Survey and the German Socio-Economic Panel, we show that variable spatial opportunity structures moderate the size of DESO at labour market entry, such that there are places where DESO disappears. Social origins assume greater importance as local labour market conditions deteriorate: in weak local labour markets, non-graduates are approximately 16 percentage points less likely to find employment if their parents are care workers rather than secondary school teachers, while graduates typically obtain first jobs that are 7–9 ISEI points lower in status. These findings highlight the distinctive geography of social stratification processes at labour market entry and potentially beyond.

articles.title: Fishing Sector Perspectives: Perceived Threats in the Polish Baltic Sea Fleet by Vessel Size and Target Species
articles.authors: Michael J. Rosciszewski-Dodgson; Giuseppe T. Cirella
articles.abstract: As ecological and economic pressures mount in the Baltic Sea, commercial fisheries face increasing challenges. This study investigates fishermen’s perceptions of four major threats—climate change, pollution, overfishing, and marine predators—with attention to vessel size (fishing boat, cutter, supercutter) and target species (demersal, mixed, small pelagic). Based on 129 surveys and 28 interviews across Poland, the analysis shows that while all threats are acknowledged, perceptions vary significantly by operational context. Seventy-eight coded interview quotations illustrate how sector-specific experiences shape these views, underscoring the need to integrate them into fisheries governance. Friedman test results (p < 0.001) confirm significant variation across threat categories, with the strongest contrasts for overfishing and predator concerns. Smaller vessels targeting demersal and mixed stocks identified predators as the primary threat, while supercutters and pelagic operators emphasized quota restrictions and international competition. Climate change and pollution were viewed as less immediate, though some reported localized impacts. Incorporating sector perspectives is essential for adaptive, sustainable fisheries management.

articles.title: Toward a Multilevel Model of Algorithmic Sociocognitive Mediation in Human-AI Interaction
articles.authors: Antonio Aguilar
articles.abstract:
Generative artificial intelligence is increasingly embedded in everyday digital environments, reshaping the conditions under which individuals perceive, interpret, validate, and respond to social information. However, current research on human-AI interaction remains fragmented across cognitive, behavioral, organizational, communicative, and sociotechnical approaches, limiting the development of integrated explanatory frameworks. This article proposes the Algorithmic Sociocognitive Mediation Model (ASM) as a multilevel theoretical framework designed to explain how algorithmic-generative systems participate in the reorganization of perception, attention, interpretation, cognition, and behavior.
The model conceptualizes algorithmic mediation as an adaptive process emerging from the interaction among four interrelated dimensions: sociotechnical structures, configurations of human-AI interaction, recursive and coadaptive feedback processes, and sociocognitive consequences. It argues that the effects of generative AI cannot be reduced to technological exposure, individual adoption, or functional automation, but must be understood through intermediate states of mediation such as algorithmic trust, cognitive delegation, attentional overload, functional dependence, critical monitoring, and behavioral regulation.
The ASM also highlights the role of algorithmic legitimacy, institutional context, decisional autonomy, personalization, and human oversight in modulating these effects. Rather than presenting generative AI as either an inherently augmenting or disruptive technology, the model identifies the configurational conditions under which algorithmic systems may support cognitive extension, self-regulation, and decision-making, or alternatively foster uncritical delegation, reduced epistemic responsibility, fatigue, dependence, and diminished autonomy. The article concludes by outlining theoretical, methodological, and normative implications for future empirical research on generative AI within complex sociotechnical ecosystems.

articles.title: Frame and Motivate: Testing strategies to increase participation in data donation studies.
articles.authors: Frieder Rodewald; Florian Keusch; Valerie Hase; Sebastian Prechsl; Frauke Kreuter; Mark Trappmann
articles.abstract:
Data protection laws in many jurisdictions grant users access to information that online platforms collect about them. Researchers capitalize on this legal requirement through data donation studies, where they ask users to request their personal data from an online platform and then share them as part of a survey. We experimentally tested two strategies to boost participation in a pre-registered study with over 2000 YouTube, Instagram or LinkedIn users from Germany: we varied (1) how we invited people, either framing the study as a web survey or directly describing it as a data donation study, and (2) the motivational appeals highlighting either prosocial advantages or personal gains from donating data.
We found that neither study framing nor motivational appeals significantly affected people's willingness to donate data or their actual donation rates. However, framing the study from the start as data donation research led to significantly more people starting the study. Our results demonstrate that researchers need to consider how they design invitations to data donation studies, while revealing that study framing or motivational appeals fail to increase respondents' willingness to donate or their actual donation behavior.

articles.title: Turning Pages into Screens: Media Adaptation and the Reclassification of Cultural Objects
articles.authors: Francesco Rampazzo; Kristopher Velasco
articles.abstract: When a cultural object moves from one medium to another, does it get reclassified, and does that reclassification change demand? We argue that it does, through a mechanism we call medium transposition: audiences evaluate objects by the conventions of the medium in front of them, so crossing media can shift the category in which an object is placed and reshape the demand that follows. We test this argument with Heartstopper, a queer young-adult graphic-novel series adapted by Netflix in April 2022, combining NielsenIQ BookData weekly sales across eleven national markets with 2,981 Amazon book reviews and 874 IMDb viewer reviews. The Netflix adaptation triggered a discontinuous increase of approximately 76,000 weekly book sales, largest in markets with dense local publishing infrastructure and running predominantly through local-language translation editions. Both audiences are responding to the same underlying story, yet they talk about it in strikingly different ways: Amazon reviewers focus on gifting and intergenerational circulation, while IMDb reviewers focus on LGBTQ+ visibility and self-recognition, a gap of 3.9 to one in representation language across platforms. A within-Amazon comparison, holding platform constant, shows that family-gift language rises and identitarian queer language declines after the Season 1 release, suggesting that the classification shift is tied to the medium crossing rather than only to differences between the two review platforms. Medium transposition offers a portable account of rapid, infrastructure-dependent, audience-driven reclassification, one that requires no shift in taste and no campaign by critics. The price, visible in the review evidence, is that expansion and dilution are not easily separated: the same process that routes Heartstopper through mainstream gatekeepers as ordinary teenage fiction also reduces the share of identitarian queer language in the discourse that surrounds it.

articles.title: Paid parenting leave from a child perspective: a pillar of sustainable development
articles.authors: Alzbeta Bartova; Margaret O'Brien; Thordis Reimer; Guðný Björk Eydal; Anna Escobedo; Cristina Castellanos Serrano; Jochen Devlieghere; Marc Grau-Grau; Philip Hwang
articles.abstract: Parenting leave policies play a crucial role in enabling children to grow up in economically secure, family-based care environments during the earliest and most sensitive stages of life. The first 1,000 days, from conception to a child’s second birthday, represent a critical developmental window in which early relationships, emotional regulation, health, and developmental trajectories are shaped. Yet parenting leave has too often been understood primarily as a labour market instrument or as support for parents, rather than as a policy that directly affects children’s rights, well-being, and life chances. This White Paper addresses this imbalance by reviewing parenting leave policies across Europe from a child-centred and child-rights perspective.

articles.title: Settlement, Return, or Onward Migration? An Intersectional Perspective on Migration Decisions
articles.authors: Katia Gallegos Torres; Agnieszka Kanas; Yuliya Kosyakova
articles.abstract: Out-migration is a central yet understudied dimension of migration processes, with important implications for labor supply, integration trajectories, and population composition. This paper examines how out-migration intentions vary across migrant groups and how economic ties, socio-cultural embeddedness, and transnational ties are associated with return and onward migration. Using recent data on immigrants in Germany, the analysis shows that out-migration intentions are shaped by multiple mechanisms rather than a single dominant factor. Socio-cultural embeddedness is the strongest correlate of lower mobility, whereas economic resources and transnational ties have more heterogeneous associations. Return and onward migration are linked to distinct configurations of attachments, and these patterns vary systematically by gender and migration motive. Men are more likely to report onward migration, and economic migrants exhibit higher overall mobility than family migrants and refugees. Overall, the findings highlight the importance of distinguishing between forms of mobility and moving beyond purely economic explanations to better understand migrant retention and mobility dynamics.

articles.title: TRANSCEND-T2D-1 and the First-Line Question: What Retatrutide's Phase 3 Results Mean for Diabetes Care, Access & Equity
articles.authors: Steven Minh Phat Duong
articles.abstract: The TRANSCEND-T2D-1 trial establishes retatrutide as the first triple agonist for type 2 diabetes. Over 40 weeks, 537 adults achieved placebo-subtracted HbA1c reductions of 0.88%–1.12% and 8.9%–12.7% (20–29 lbs) weight loss, exceeding tirzepatide's weight loss in SURPASS-1 and rivaling bariatric surgery. Yet retatrutide's efficacy comes with higher adverse events (discontinuation 2.5% overall, 5.1% for 12 mg vs. 0% placebo), a projected annual cost of $12,000, and a budget impact ICER estimates would exceed $880 million annually with <1% eligible patients treated. This creates a hidden equity mechanism: patients with paid sick leave can navigate dose escalation; those without cannot. Existing GLP-1 disparities forewarn. Non-Hispanic Blacks have 28% lower odds of GLP-1 prescription, and Black and Hispanic patients face lower out-of-pocket costs and lower fill rates. An unregulated gray market already flourishes: 15 pounds of retatrutide entered the U.S. between Jan–Apr 2026, with 14 hospitalizations reported. Three policy interventions, CMS Coverage with Evidence Development, FDA post-marketing diversity requirements, and state Medicaid VBP, could improve access if implemented before FDA approval, expected within 12-18 months. The next 18 months will determine whether this breakthrough succeeds or becomes a marker of who can afford the best care.

articles.title: Is it all about the costs? Cultural distance and adjustment of recent migrants
articles.authors: Andreas Genoni; Jean Philippe Décieux; Elke Murdock
articles.abstract: The cultural distance (CD) between the origin and destination country of migrants has long been considered important for individual adjustment. Past research predominantly followed a cost perspective, emphasising rising adjustment costs with increasing CD and postulating a negative linear relationship. Previous studies therefore overlooked potential benefits of CD for adjustment. We address this gap by taking a more differentiated perspective on CD and adjustment. Using probability-based survey data on recent German migrants , we examined migrant adjustment at different CD-levels. Employing multilevel regressions for sociocultural and psychological adjustment, the findings reveal an inverted-U shaped relationship between CD and both adjustment dimensions. This challenges the cost perspective, indicating an “optimal” range of intermediate CD as most beneficial for adjustment. Thus, the prevailing view on negative consequences of CD for adjustment may underestimate the challenges for culturally close migrants, and overlook that certain levels of cultural dissimilarity can facilitate migrants’ adjustment.

articles.title: Disfungsi Pengawasan Badan Permusyawaratan Desa: Subordinasi Struktural, Asimetri Informasi, dan Desain Kelembagaan Alternatif
articles.authors: Sahroni Darmawan
articles.abstract: The Village Consultative Council (BPD) was designed under Law 6/2014, amended by Law 3/2024, as an institutional counterweight to the village head. In practice, it operates in dysfunction due to structural subordination and fiscal dependency. Through policy analysis of Law 3/2024, GP 16/2026, and MoHA Regulations 110/2016 and 20/2018, this article identifies seven design failures, including budget dependency, recruitment vulnerabilities, weak enforcement, and information asymmetry. Comparative analysis with local councils in India, Bangladesh, and the Philippines reveals that oversight independence requires protected budgets and proactive information access. This article recommends three policy innovations via regional discretion without statutory amendment: a protected budget mechanism (10–15% of the Village Fund Allocation), a proactive right-to-receive information framework, and an institutionalized Village Social Audit Forum integrated with regional inspectorates and measured through a Village Democracy Health Index (VDHI).  Keywords: village governance, BPD oversight, institutional design, social audit, Village Fund Allocation

articles.title: Aspirational Mathematics and the Behavioral Sciences: The Columbia Behavioral Models Project, 1952–1957
articles.authors: Jefferson Pooley
articles.abstract: The Behavioral Models project (1952–1957) at Columbia University had a clear mission: to make mathematics legible to social scientists. The project, funded by the Office of Naval Research (ONR), enlisted mathematicians and philosophers to demonstrate how mathematical models could be applied to the study of human behavior. Measured against this aim, the project was a failure. The flavors of mathematical modeling promoted by the project were rarely adopted by mainstream social scientists, the target audience. If anything, the disciplines embraced (or re-embraced) the correlational tradition of inferential statistics—an approach that the project had defined itself against. The paper argues that the Behavioral Models Project (BMP) is an expression of aspirational mathematics. The postwar behavioral sciences movement imagined itself, in a gauzy, rhetorical sense, in the image of the natural sciences. But its more proximate reference discipline was economics, which was already self-segregating by the early 1950s. Economists’ mix of mathematical rigor and policy influence, on early and notably partial display in these years, made their discipline an object of envy and qualified emulation. The BMP—framed as a maths booster shot for the behavioral sciences—was a high-profile index of that dynamic. Mathematical sophistication, if not necessarily the economists’ variety, was something to cultivate. As the BMP case helps to show, the aspiration went unfulfilled.

articles.title: Focused MAIHDA: Studying Intersectional Inequalities through the Lens of a Focal Social Identity
articles.authors: George Leckie
articles.abstract: Intersectional MAIHDA (Multilevel Analysis of Individual Heterogeneity and Discriminatory Accuracy) is a widely used approach in quantitative intersectional research for exploring how mean outcomes vary across combinations of social identity characteristics, such as gender, race, and social class. However, in many studies, researchers are especially interested in a particular identity, such as gender, operationalised (imperfectly) by sex. This gives rise to additional questions that cannot be addressed using standard MAIHDA: Does the magnitude and importance of intersectional inequalities differ between men and women? If so, who experiences greater inequality? To what extent do additive patterns of intersectional inequality align across the sexes, and where do they differ? Are departures from these general patterns, reflecting unique intersectional experiences, more pronounced for one sex than the other? Finally, at the level of specific strata, where do the largest sex differences emerge? To address these questions, we introduce a focused-lens approach to MAIHDA that enables researchers to investigate situations in which sex—or any other social identity—is of particular interest. In doing so, we expand the range of questions that MAIHDA can address and thereby broaden its contribution to quantitative intersectional research. We motivate and illustrate this approach by revisiting the seminal BMI MAIHDA application of Evans et al. (2018).

articles.title: The Red Queen at the Grassroots: The Co-Evolution of Cadre Resistance and  Regime Control
articles.authors: Xuanyuan Li
articles.abstract:
Why do Chinese grassroots cadres sometimes voluntarily report their own 
disciplinary violations, triggering investigations that suspend their impossible policy 
tasks? And what happens when the disciplinary system learns to strike back? Drawing 
on 32 in-depth interviews with current and former cadres in S City, H Province, 
combined with official statistics and policy documents (2020–2026), this paper 
documents an emerging phenomenon:"cadre self-informing",and traces its evolution 
from a rational risk-avoidance strategy to a failing one.
Self-informing becomes rational under a triple condition: fiscal depletion 
(local self-sufficiency rate below 35%), incentive inversion (the penalty for 
"incompetence" outweighs that for "corruption"), and the collapse of social trust. 
Facing zero-budget demolition tasks and catastrophic sanctions for failure, cadres 
strategically exploit a procedural rigidity,that every disciplinary report must be 
investigated,to pause the implementation clock and seek refuge in a bounded, 
predictable investigation rather than an unbounded, career-ending charge of 
incompetence.
Yet the system does not remain inert. Through longitudinal tracking of a key 
case,a cadre whose self-informing strategy survived a traditional investigation but 
unraveled spectacularly under an AI-enhanced one,this paper reveals that the 
disciplinary apparatus is itself a learning entity. AI-driven tools compressed 
investigation timelines, shattered the informational opacity "battle-tested" cadres had 
relied upon, and fundamentally recalibrated the risk calculus of self-informing.
Drawing on the Red Queen effect, this paper reconceptualizes the "execution 
limit" of China's pressure-based system not as a static boundary, but as a moving front 
in an ongoing arms race between agents and the institutions that discipline them. The 
findings advance institutional gap theory beyond state-society contention into internal 
regime-agent dynamics, and identify a "high-pressure trap" in adaptive 
authoritarianism: over-mobilization drains the very resources and trust that 
adaptability requires, generating the evasion it seeks to extinguish.

articles.title: Rethinking Certification Culture in Procurement and Supply Chain: Why Capability Must Replace Credentialism in the Age of AI
articles.authors: Alaa Khodroj
articles.abstract: Certification bodies have long shaped the professional landscape of procurement and supply chain, offering standardized knowledge and global recognition. Yet the increasing reliance on theoretical exams, recurring fees, and credential centric hiring has created structural challenges that limit inclusivity, undervalue experience, and fail to reflect the realities of modern practice. This paper examines the limitations of certification driven evaluation, the social and economic pressures it creates, and the widening gap between certification standards and real world capability — especially as artificial intelligence now performs many tasks traditionally tested in certification exams. Rather than dismissing certification bodies, the paper proposes a shift toward capability based, evidence driven, AI enhanced validation that honors practical judgment, ethical decision making, and lived professional experience. The goal is reform, not rejection: a modernized model where certification bodies evolve from exam providers into capability verification authorities.

articles.title: The Structural Failure of Passive Observation and Recovery by Source-Independent Action: Minimal Results in a Finite, Elementary Setting
articles.authors: ZHIKE ZHANG
articles.abstract: Using only elementary finite mathematics—the pigeonhole principle and the finite law of total probability, with no causal graph, no measure theory, and no axiom of infinity—we characterise when passive observation suffices to predict interventions and when it does not, obtaining three results. First, impossibility: every bounded representation f : X → Z with |X| > |Z| satisfying a sufficient-statistic condition admits a pair of environments that are observationally identical yet interventionally distinct, on which every passive strategy fails at every sample size. The obstruction performs no arithmetic on probabilities—distributions enter only through equality and its negation, so it holds verbatim with distributions replaced by abstract labels; arithmetic enters exactly once, a single inequality converting the failure into error floors uniform in the sample size. Second, sufficiency: under a source-invariance condition on the outcome mechanism, any non-adaptive policy that draws each action value from a source independent of the latent state recovers the interventional distribution by a single finite sum. Third, necessity: among fixed assignment sources, source independence is exactly the condition for recovery uniform over outcome mechanisms, and every dependent source manufactures the same two-point obstruction, with worst-case error equal to the dependence of the source. Together these give a tight two-sided characterisation: source-independent action is necessary and sufficient for recovery, a dependent source is as uninformative as passive observation, and source independence is what separates failure from recovery. The action operation is graph-free, coinciding with Pearl's do on acyclic structural causal models and extending to simultaneous-equilibrium environments on which do is undefined.

articles.title: Notes on the categorial-logic of Capital
articles.authors: Lucas Carvalho Peto
articles.abstract: The validity of the “method applied in Capital” as an external toolkit is one of the main issues that follows the publication of the first volume in 1867. The proposal presented here is based on the premise that this problem rests on a basic formal misunderstanding of the Marxian legacy. The hypothesis herein is as follows: the presentation found in Capital imposes that the method must conform to the object by the latter’s necessity.

articles.title: Turning Pages into Screens: Media Adaptation and the Reclassification of Cultural Objects
articles.authors: Francesco Rampazzo; Kristopher Velasco
articles.abstract: When a cultural object moves from one medium to another, does it get reclassified, and does that reclassification change demand? We argue that it does, through a mechanism we call medium transposition: audiences evaluate objects by the conventions of the medium in front of them, so crossing media can shift the category in which an object is placed and reshape the demand that follows. We test this argument with Heartstopper, a queer young-adult graphic-novel series adapted by Netflix in April 2022, combining NielsenIQ BookData weekly sales across eleven national markets with 2,981 Amazon book reviews and 874 IMDb viewer reviews. The Netflix adaptation triggered a discontinuous increase of approximately 76,000 weekly book sales, largest in markets with dense local publishing infrastructure and running predominantly through local-language translation editions. Both audiences are responding to the same underlying story, yet they talk about it in strikingly different ways: Amazon reviewers focus on gifting and intergenerational circulation, while IMDb reviewers focus on LGBTQ+ visibility and self-recognition, a gap of 3.9 to one in representation language across platforms. A within-Amazon comparison, holding platform constant, shows that family-gift language rises and identitarian queer language declines after the Season 1 release, suggesting that the classification shift is tied to the medium crossing rather than only to differences between the two review platforms. Medium transposition offers a portable account of rapid, infrastructure-dependent, audience-driven reclassification, one that requires no shift in taste and no campaign by critics. The price, visible in the review evidence, is that expansion and dilution are not easily separated: the same process that routes Heartstopper through mainstream gatekeepers as ordinary teenage fiction also reduces the share of identitarian queer language in the discourse that surrounds it.

articles.title: Challengers Shift to Lower-Tier Competition When Elite Incumbents Extend Their Tenure: Evidence From Professional Tennis
articles.authors: Kenny Ching
articles.abstract:
Extended incumbent tenure is hard to study empirically because dominant incumbents and weaker successors may simply differ in talent. We study this using professional tennis, where the
Big Four—Federer, Nadal, Djokovic, and Murray—remained at the summit 5–10 years beyond the historical norm. For 296 players with fully elapsed prime windows, we construct a tenure
extension exposure index measuring how far past historical norms the Big Four were during each player’s prime. Controlling for pre-prime talent, higher exposure predicts fewer main-tour matches, more Challenger-circuit matches, and earlier exit from the world top 100 once secular trends are absorbed—consistent with downward substitution from elite to sub-elite competition. Raw Big Four dominance shows the opposite pattern, indicating the effect is specific to extended tenure, not opponent quality. Talented athletes are not simply outcompeted but redirected to lower-visibility, lower-revenue tiers, with implications for tournament design, athlete welfare, and succession planning where senior careers are lengthening.

articles.title: South Korea’s Nuclear-Energy Entanglements and the Timescales of Ecological Democracy
articles.authors: Nan Kim
articles.abstract:
This chapter analyzes controversies surrounding nuclear-energy infrastructure in South Korea, where
the density of nuclear reactors is the highest in the world. Focusing on the city of Kyŏngju – a UNESCO World
Heritage site closely associated with both South Korean cultural patrimony and the authoritarian
developmentalism that drove the expansion of the country’s nuclear-energy program – it asks how democratic
thinking and practice reckon with the inheritance of energy infrastructure from the military-authoritarian era. The
chapter examines how two unexpected earthquakes in 2016, occurring only months after the opening of Asia’s
first underground repository for low- and intermediate-level radioactive waste, brought renewed scrutiny to the
contingent futures of nuclear energy. Though not causally related, this improbable convergence exposed tensions
concerning technocratic risk management, democratic deliberation, and the long temporal horizons of radioactive
waste stewardship. Situating these debates within the post-authoritarian politics of nuclear governance, the
chapter argues that nuclear infrastructure poses ethical and political challenges that exceed the compressed
timelines of technocratic governance and policy-making. In light of the multimillennial timescales of the nuclear
fuel cycle, the chapter explores how ecological democracy might reframe risk and responsibility within larger
questions of intergenerational accountability in the nuclear Anthropocene.

articles.title: Artificial Intelligence at the crossroads: Productivity J-curve and carbon trade-offs in the global economy
articles.authors: Soumyajit Bhunia; Sneha Dey; Priya Mondal
articles.abstract:
Artificial intelligence functions as an engine for increasing the productivity and economic growth of a nation. At the same time, it creates economic inequalities. The rapid development of the AI sector has profoundly transformed global systems, fundamentally altered operational paradigms, and paved novel pathways for economic growth. This study is trying to examine the intricate relationship between AI and economic growth, particularly on both the productivity gains and economic inequalities associated with AI adoption. We frame this scenario through the lens of productivity J-curve analysis. Using a panel of selected OECD countries spanning a period of eight years (2013-2020), the present study seeks to shed some light to examine the association among technological, political, and environmental factors, particularly AI patents and robot installation and their effects on productivity, measured by Total Factor Productivity (TFP). Furthermore, this study also focuses on the complex relationship between political factors, specifically democracy and political stability, and their impact on environmental degradation, measured by carbon emissions. To assess this relationship, we apply a novel econometric technique, the Seemingly Unrelated Regression Equation (SURE) estimation approach. 
Findings from our empirical study support the productivity J-curve hypothesis, where AI adoption at the early phase exhibits slower productivity growth, and the organizational changes catch up over time. It highlights the necessity for sustained organizational adaptation, realizing the full productivity gain of emerging technologies. Democratic systems frequently face delays in enacting and enforcing stringent environmental regulations due to political negotiations. Governments may prioritize economic development, often postponing environmental regulations to boost economic growth.

articles.title: Risk Environments of Postsocialism. Sex Work, Drug Policy, and HIV in Eastern and Southeastern Europe Between War, Criminalisation, and External Defunding. A Theoretical Synthesis.
articles.authors: Susanne Bleier Wilp
articles.abstract:
This paper develops a theoretical synthesis of sex work, drug policy and HIV in Eastern
and Southeastern Europe - a region that fits neither the governance typology of the
Global North nor the self-organisation typology of the Global South developed in two
companion papers. The region's defining feature is the intersection of sex work with
injecting drug use under double criminalisation, governed in much of the post-Soviet
space not by criminal law in the strict sense but by a distinct administrative-offence
regime inherited from the late Soviet period. Eastern Europe and Central Asia is,
alongside the Middle East/North Africa and Latin America, one of three world regions
where new HIV infections have risen rather than fallen since 2010. Two axes structure
the analysis. The first traces the postsocialist legal-epidemiological complex from its
administrative-law foundations through Tim Rhodes' risk environment framework -
developed in this region - to the catastrophic 2014 Crimean methadone ban and the
contrasting resilience of Ukraine's wartime opioid agonist treatment system. The second
traces an inverted extraterritorial axis: where the Northern paper found US power
exercised through platform law and the Southern paper through the PEPFAR pledge,
this region experiences the abrupt 2025 withdrawal of PEPFAR/USAID funding colliding
with an indigenous Russian counter-power - 'traditional values' politics and foreign-agent
legislation that actively suppresses sex worker self-organisation (SWAN, Silver Rose, Legalife-Ukraine) rather than merely
leaving it unfunded. A cross-cutting axis examines Southeastern Europe and intra-EU
mobility, where Romania's collapse from Global Fund eligibility to antiretroviral supply
crises shows treatment access becoming a function of residency and funding status
rather than medical need - a pattern this paper extends to the underexamined situation
of HIV-positive sex workers who return from Western Europe to Romania or Bulgaria.
The paper argues that the postsocialist risk environment constitutes a fourth, structurally
distinct case: neither self-built protective infrastructure as in the South nor
institutionalised labour-law normalisation as in the North, but a terrain where both state
and self-organised protection remain precarious against external shocks - war, funding
withdrawal, and authoritarian repression alike.

https://doi.org/10.31235/osf.io/ckru6_v1

https://doi.org/10.31235/osf.io/uc57b_v1

articles.title: Risikoumfelder des Postsozialismus. Sexarbeit, Drogenpolitik und HIV in Ost- und Südosteuropa zwischen Krieg, Kriminalisierung und externem Mittelentzug. Eine theoretische Synthese
articles.authors: Susanne Bleier Wilp
articles.abstract:
This paper develops a theoretical synthesis of sex work, drug policy and HIV in Eastern
and Southeastern Europe - a region that fits neither the governance typology of the
Global North nor the self-organisation typology of the Global South developed in two
companion papers. The region's defining feature is the intersection of sex work with
injecting drug use under double criminalisation, governed in much of the post-Soviet
space not by criminal law in the strict sense but by a distinct administrative-offence
regime inherited from the late Soviet period. Eastern Europe and Central Asia is,
alongside the Middle East/North Africa and Latin America, one of three world regions
where new HIV infections have risen rather than fallen since 2010. Two axes structure
the analysis. The first traces the postsocialist legal-epidemiological complex from its
administrative-law foundations through Tim Rhodes' risk environment framework -
developed in this region - to the catastrophic 2014 Crimean methadone ban and the
contrasting resilience of Ukraine's wartime opioid agonist treatment system. The second
traces an inverted extraterritorial axis: where the Northern paper found US power
exercised through platform law and the Southern paper through the PEPFAR pledge,
this region experiences the abrupt 2025 withdrawal of PEPFAR/USAID funding colliding
with an indigenous Russian counter-power - 'traditional values' politics and foreign-agent
legislation that actively suppresses sex worker self-organisation (SWAN, Silver Rose,
Legalife-Ukraine) rather than merely leaving it unfunded. A cross-cutting axis examines
Southeastern Europe and intra-EU mobility, where Romania's collapse from Global Fund eligibility to
antiretroviral supply crises shows treatment access becoming a function of residency
and funding status rather than medical need - a pattern this paper extends to the
underexamined situation of HIV-positive sex workers who return from Western Europe to
Romania or Bulgaria. The paper argues that the postsocialist risk environment
constitutes a fourth, structurally distinct case: neither self-built protective infrastructure
as in the South nor institutionalised labour-law normalisation as in the North, but a
terrain where both state and self-organised protection remain precarious against
external shocks - war, funding withdrawal, and authoritarian repression alike.

articles.title: The Red Queen at the Grassroots: The Co-Evolution of Cadre Resistance and  Regime Control
articles.authors: Xuanyuan Li
articles.abstract:
Why do Chinese grassroots cadres sometimes voluntarily report their own 
disciplinary violations, triggering investigations that suspend their impossible policy 
tasks? And what happens when the disciplinary system learns to strike back? Drawing 
on 32 in-depth interviews with current and former cadres in S City, H Province, 
combined with official statistics and policy documents (2020–2026), this paper 
documents an emerging phenomenon:"cadre self-informing",and traces its evolution 
from a rational risk-avoidance strategy to a failing one.
Self-informing becomes rational under a triple condition: fiscal depletion 
(local self-sufficiency rate below 35%), incentive inversion (the penalty for 
"incompetence" outweighs that for "corruption"), and the collapse of social trust. 
Facing zero-budget demolition tasks and catastrophic sanctions for failure, cadres 
strategically exploit a procedural rigidity,that every disciplinary report must be 
investigated,to pause the implementation clock and seek refuge in a bounded, 
predictable investigation rather than an unbounded, career-ending charge of 
incompetence.
Yet the system does not remain inert. Through longitudinal tracking of a key 
case,a cadre whose self-informing strategy survived a traditional investigation but 
unraveled spectacularly under an AI-enhanced one,this paper reveals that the 
disciplinary apparatus is itself a learning entity. AI-driven tools compressed 
investigation timelines, shattered the informational opacity "battle-tested" cadres had 
relied upon, and fundamentally recalibrated the risk calculus of self-informing.
Drawing on the Red Queen effect, this paper reconceptualizes the "execution 
limit" of China's pressure-based system not as a static boundary, but as a moving front 
in an ongoing arms race between agents and the institutions that discipline them. The 
findings advance institutional gap theory beyond state-society contention into internal 
regime-agent dynamics, and identify a "high-pressure trap" in adaptive 
authoritarianism: over-mobilization drains the very resources and trust that 
adaptability requires, generating the evasion it seeks to extinguish.

articles.title: Housing Commodification and Structural Dilemmas:  An Ethnographic Study of "Atypical Street-Sleepers" in Hong Kong
articles.authors: Xuanyuan Li
articles.abstract:
Hong Kong's status as a global financial hub, with its iconic skyline and 
capital concentration, belies a reality hidden in its urban interstices—under 
footbridges, in backstreets, and in all-night fast-food outlets—where a population of 
"street-sleepers" resides. These individuals are not homeless in the conventional sense 
of lacking shelter. Rather, they are often people who, under Hong Kong’s distinct 
socio-economic pressures, actively or passively adopt the streets as their "beds" and 
public spaces as their "homes."
Drawing on fieldwork conducted between October 2024 and April 2025 in 
districts such as Prince Edward, Sham Shui Po, and Mong Kok East, this ethnographic 
study examines how institutional fragility in a global financial city reshapes life 
trajectories through housing commodification, leading to homelessness. Using a 
mixed-methods approach—including quantitative analysis of Sham Shui Po data, 
participatory ethnography, and life-history reconstruction of informants like "Auntie" 
and "Zhong Bo"—the research identifies a triple mechanism of risk transmission: 
policy discontinuity, family support disintegration, and employment polarization.
The findings reveal that for many, street-sleeping is not an absolute "loss," but 
a passive "choice" amid systemic failure—a "shelter" for escaping trauma and 
preserving dignity. Ultimately, this paper argues that homelessness in Hong Kong 
stems from the convergence of its neoliberal governance, financialized housing 
regime, and residual welfare system. These life stories collectively depict how 
individuals are engulfed by systemic vulnerabilities, rendering this study not only a 
record of a marginalized group but also a critique of the Hong Kong development 
model and its human cost.

articles.title: Challenges to intergroup cultural transmission in humans
articles.authors: Patricio Cruz y Celis Peniche; Cristina Moya
articles.abstract: Despite its ubiquity, the spread of cultural traits across group boundaries is not easily reconciled with many cultural transmission biases that encourage stability rather than change. Adaptation to local environments and coordination costs should motivate parochial and conformist learning. It seems surprising that humans would ever benefit from copying outgroups, particularly when such models are rare, and copying them can be penalized by one’s ingroup. While we readily copy better traits or successful demonstrators, including from outgroup models, indicators of success are often group-specific and harder to observe in outgroups, reducing their perceived value. As such, it is unclear how learners evaluate outgroup traits while minimizing the risks of copying the wrong trait, copying the right trait wrongly, or being punished by their ingroup regardless. In this review we address how and why intergroup cultural transmission (or IGCT) happens despite its potential pitfalls, and how learners manage these risks. We propose a functional taxonomy of forces affecting IGCT that considers the role of non-normative versus normative costs, informational costs, and whether traits’ payoffs are frequency-dependent. We review formal models and experimental, ethnographic, and historical case studies that illustrate these dynamics, ending with a discussion of unanswered questions.

articles.title: Challenges to intergroup cultural transmission in humans
articles.authors: Patricio Cruz y Celis Peniche; Cristina Moya
articles.abstract: Despite its ubiquity, the spread of cultural traits across group boundaries is not easily reconciled with many cultural transmission biases that encourage stability rather than change. Adaptation to local environments and coordination costs should motivate parochial and conformist learning. It seems surprising that humans would ever benefit from copying outgroups, particularly when such models are rare, and copying them can be penalized by one’s ingroup. While we readily copy better traits or successful demonstrators, including from outgroup models, indicators of success are often group-specific and harder to observe in outgroups, reducing their perceived value. As such, it is unclear how learners evaluate outgroup traits while minimizing the risks of copying the wrong trait, copying the right trait wrongly, or being punished by their ingroup regardless. In this review we address how and why intergroup cultural transmission (or IGCT) happens despite its potential pitfalls, and how learners manage these risks. We propose a functional taxonomy of forces affecting IGCT that considers the role of non-normative versus normative costs, informational costs, and whether traits’ payoffs are frequency-dependent. We review formal models and experimental, ethnographic, and historical case studies that illustrate these dynamics, ending with a discussion of unanswered questions.

articles.title: A Stylized Computable General Equilibrium Model for Circular Economy Strategy Analysis
articles.authors: Riccardo Boero
articles.abstract: This work presents a stylized computable general equilibrium (CGE) model for analysing circular economy policy strategies in an intentionally abstract setting. The model is not calibrated to a specific economy; instead, it uses round-number calibration data and systematic numerical experiments to identify the behavioural and physical conditions under which circular strategies reduce primary material use, represented by virgin metal. The analysis compares virgin-metal taxation, recycling support, and life-extension support through refurbishment, repair, and reuse in single-country and two-country model variants. The results show that circular strategies cannot be ranked by label alone. Virgin-metal taxation operates through an upstream material-price channel and is comparatively insensitive to how end-of-life products are allocated among circular routes. Recycling and life-extension support depend more strongly on collection and allocation of end-of-life products, route yields, recycled-metal quality, route substitutability, and final-product service-demand response. For some combinations of demand elasticity, substitution elasticities, route yields, and recycled-metal quality, a policy can reduce virgin-metal use even while demand for the final product service increases; for others, the same demand response can weaken or reverse the expected primary-material reduction. Support-efficiency comparisons show that refurbishment support performs better than recycling support in the closed-economy case, while this gap narrows when virgin-metal production is separated from consumption and circular processing in the two-country extension. The nested life-extension analysis further shows that refurbishment, repair, and reuse are not interchangeable policy mechanisms. Overall, the model provides a reproducible equilibrium framework for identifying where circular economy strategies work, where they fail, and which assumptions drive the difference.

articles.title: From Digital Public Infrastructure to AI Governance: Lessons from India for the Global South
articles.authors: Aditi Singh Tharran
articles.abstract:
Digital Public Infrastructure, or DPI, has become the model that governments
across the Global South are told to copy. India's Aadhaar identity system and its
Unified Payments Interface sit at the center of that story. Now that the same
governments are confronting artificial intelligence, a second claim has attached
itself to the first: that whatever institutional strength built India's DPI should also
help a state govern AI. This paper puts that claim to the test instead of taking it on
faith. It compares India, Brazil, Indonesia and Kenya using a most-differentsystems design, tracing six candidate mechanisms through which DPI experience
might generate AI governance capacity, to ask where that capacity actually comes
from. Three rival hypotheses are weighed against the record: direct institutional
transfer, technological discontinuity, and conditional adaptation. The evidence
favours the third. DPI-derived capacity does carry over, but only in part, and only
once it meets independent regulators, enforceable data-protection law and
accountability mechanisms that DPI programmes do not generate on their own. To
keep the claim testable, the paper treats AI governance capacity as a
disaggregated construct covering regulatory, enforcement, technical, coordination,
accountability, data-governance and compute capacity, and builds a DPI-to-AI
Governance Framework that specifies when transfer works and when it does not. It
ends with what this means for governments drawn to the "India model," and with
an agenda for the comparative and quantitative work that should follow.

articles.title: From the integration paradox to migration aspirations:  Return migration, onward migration, or immobility?
articles.authors: Yuliya Kosyakova; Nils Witte
articles.abstract: Despite extensive research on the integration paradox, its behavioral consequences remain largely unexamined. Although integration is expected to foster long-term settlement, structurally integrated migrants often report heightened discrimination. We argue that these experiences increase aspirations to leave, but their translation into migration plans depends on migrants’ capabilities. Building on an aspirations–capabilities framework, we conceptualize education as a key determinant of migration capabilities distinguishing between absolute and relative education. Absolute education captures skill transferability across destinations, whereas relative education reflects migrants’ position within the origin-country distribution and associated status expectations. We further argue that return and onward migration follow distinct evaluative logics: return is shaped by relative status considerations vis-à-vis the origin country, while onward migration reflects perceived skill transferability in alternative destinations. Using data from the International Mobility Panel of Migrants (IMPa), a large-scale survey of immigrants in Germany (N=41,600), we show migrants with higher absolute education are more likely to respond to alienation by planning onward migration. Contrary to our expectations, higher relative education is also associated with increased onward migration, while those with lower relative education are more likely to consider return.

articles.title: The Temporal Turn in Social Movement Studies: Political Readings of Temporality
articles.authors: Nerea Montejo-López
articles.abstract: The study of temporality has gained increasing prominence in sociology and social movement studies. As this wave of research on temporality has risen, scholars have increasingly argued that time should not be understood as a neutral backdrop but as a dimension of power and conflict. Building on the concept of the capitalist timescape as a socio‐political regime that organises, synchronises, and commodifies time through dynamics of acceleration and permanent crisis, this emerging critical field shows how temporal logics discipline everyday rhythms, compress horizons of political action, and foreclose alternative futures. Fighting against the capitalist temporal order is a battlefield of social movements which do not simply exist in time but act upon it, generating counter‐temporalities that reconfigure pasts, presents, and futures. By explicitly bridging the sociology of time and social movement studies, this article shows that the temporal turn cannot be understood without situating social movements within broader socio‐political regimes of temporality. Recent scholarship has articulated this “temporal turn” in Social Movement Studies, highlighting how social movements mobilise concepts such as critical junctures, memory, rhythm, anticipation, and prefiguration to contest the capitalist timescape. While contributions have multiplied in recent years, they remain fragmented across these different strands. This article provides an analytical overview of this literature and calls for a more integrated framework to capture how affective, symbolic, and practical practices of temporal agency intersect. It argues that grasping time politically is crucial to deconstruct the capitalist temporal order, and strengthen the strategies of resistance that emerge against it.

articles.title: Escape or Attack? Chinese “Nan niang” Subculture on Bilibili:  Gender Performativity and the Cost of Belonging
articles.authors: Xuanyuan Li
articles.abstract:
In China‘s official discourse, the promotion of “masculine vigor” has intensified 
in recent years. Yet, on Bilibili, a video platform anchored in ACG culture, the “Nan 
niang” phenomenon:biologically male individuals performing highly feminized 
identities,is flourishing. This contradiction reveals a strategic retreat among young 
Chinese men facing the collapse of hegemonic masculinity, mounting pressures in the 
marriage market, and post-pandemic economic precarity. Drawing on systematic 
observation of 12 “Nan niang” influencers and discourse analysis of approximately 
20,000 user comments, this study reveals that the “Nan niang” phenomenon and the 
Western manosphere constitute mirror responses to the same social pressures: the 
former turns inward, performing femininity to dissolve masculine anxiety; the latter 
turns outward, attacking women to reclaim a hyper-masculine identity. Bilibili’s ACG 
culture and “moe” mechanism depoliticize and aestheticize gender transgression, 
providing a unique “gender comfort zone” for Nan niang practitioners. The paper 
proposes two localized concepts: the “cost of belonging” (the economic and 
psychological price men pay to be recognized as “qualified men”) and 
“escapism-through-community” (the strategy by which subcultural groups 
collectively evade real-world pressures), offering a new analytical framework for 
understanding marginalized groups’ digital survival strategies in a risk society.

articles.title: Epistemic injustice in scientometric analysis of African academic literature.  A Foucauldian discourse analysis of limitation texts
articles.authors: Patricia Alonso-Álvarez
articles.abstract: Scientometric analyses are widely used to study research, yet they often rely on global citation databases with uneven coverage of the certain countries and regions. This study examines how such limitations are addressed within the literature itself. Using Foucauldian Discourse Analysis, a corpus of scientometric studies on African research is analysed to explore how authors construct African science, justify methodological choices, and position themselves in relation to database exclusions. The analysis shows that African scholarship is frequently rendered partially invisible, framed as a hypothetical risk, or naturalized as an inevitable limitation of bibliometric data. At the same time, discourses of excellence and methodological authority are mobilized to sustain the validity of these studies while deferring responsibility for inclusivity to future research. These practices may reproduce forms of meta-insensitive or active ignorance, contributing to contributory epistemic injustice in representations of African science.

articles.title: Toward Human-Centered Behavioral Sustainability: A Welfare-Based Framework for Sustainable Behavior
articles.authors: Anton Oleynov
articles.abstract: Contemporary sustainability debates increasingly recognize that ecological crises cannot be understood solely through models of rational utility maximization and growth-centered development. However, existing sustainability frameworks — including happiness economics, capability theory, social capital research, and behavioral nudging — inadequately explain how non-material welfare conditions shape the long-term reproduction of sustainable practices. This paper develops the concept of behavioral sustainability, defined as the durable reproduction of sustainable patterns of behavior through supportive emotional, relational, cultural, and institutional conditions. Drawing on behavioral economics, well-being research, sustainability studies, and social practice theory, the paper proposes three complementary welfare dimensions — emotional utility, relational utility, and cultural utility — that extend conventional material conceptions of utility and identify the mechanisms through which sustainable practices become self-reinforcing rather than fragile. Using Thailand as a theoretically informative illustrative case, the analysis examines how cultural norms such as sabai-sabai, kreng jai, and nam jai, and the Sufficiency Economy Philosophy, support understandings of well-being grounded in moderation, social attachment, and emotional ease. The paper argues that behavioral sustainability is achieved when individuals can meet needs for security, recognition, and meaning through non-material sources rather than expanding consumption — a condition the paper calls welfare substitution. The framework contributes to sustainability theory by specifying this missing mechanism and by advancing human-centered sustainability as an evaluative standard that assesses development according to its capacity to sustain human flourishing alongside ecological resilience.

articles.title: Sequencing Peace: How Patterns of Cooperation Shape Public Opinion on Rapprochement
articles.authors: Jungmin Han; Thomas Chadefaux
articles.abstract: How does the trajectory of cooperation from a foreign adversary shape public support for peace? Existing research conceptualizes cooperative signals observed through rapprochement as isolated events, yet citizens often assess an adversary's behavior retrospectively and use these temporal records to infer future intentions. We argue that these cooperation trajectories matter because, even when rapprochement consists of positive interactions overall, negative changes in cooperation can erode public evaluations by resonating with prior beliefs that the adversary is inherently hostile. Thus, citizens exposed to temporal patterns in which cooperation deteriorates or alternates with decline form lower expectations of future cooperation and express stronger resistance to peace initiatives. We test this argument using two survey experiments: a hypothetical U.S.–China rapprochement scenario in the South China Sea (N = 2,324) and a selective-history experiment examining the 2018 inter-Korean rapprochement in South Korea (N = 2,000). Across both studies, respondents exposed to inconsistent or descending cooperation trajectories anticipated lower levels of future adversarial cooperation and showed less support for conciliatory policies than those exposed to alternative trajectories or cooperation without a discernible pattern. These findings explain why rapprochement that features exchanges of meaningful concessions often fails to secure the public endorsement necessary for durable peace.

articles.title: External News Efficacy: Introducing an understudied mediator in news trust choices
articles.authors: Augusto Valeriani; Diego Garusi; Gaetano Scaduto
articles.abstract: Research shows that unmet journalistic expectations undermine trust, yet the cognitive mechanisms linking the two remain underexplored. As new journalistic actors and formats reshape journalist–audience relationships, this becomes an important area of inquiry. Drawing on external efficacy research, we introduce “external news efficacy”, defined as individuals’ perception that journalists are accessible, disclosive in their decision-making processes, caring, influenceable by, and receptive to ordinary people. We hypothesize that the construct mediates the relationship between journalistic expectation–evaluation discrepancies and trust in news media. Using a structural equation model on a representative sample of Italian adults, results show that such discrepancies reduce trust partly by lowering external news efficacy.

articles.title: Informal Rules as Regulatory Ecosystems: Integration, Disintegration, Drift, and Fragmentation in Regulatory Governance Systems
articles.authors: Michael McGuinness
articles.abstract: Regulatory governance is formally designed through rules, mandates, and procedures, yet enacted through informal rule systems that shape how authority is interpreted in practice. This article develops the Informal Rules Ecosystem Model, explaining how informal norms, tacit routines, social expectations, and informal enforcement mechanisms interact to produce system level dynamics. The model accounts for integration, disintegration, drift, and fragmentation, showing why formally similar arrangements generate divergent execution time behaviour. Using a conceptual model building approach and a multi agency regulatory illustration, the article demonstrates how informal rule ecosystems shape information sharing, joint enforcement, and threshold interpretation across regulatory settings.

articles.title: Transforming Regulatory Systems: A Seven-Layer Architecture for Public Sector Capability, Stewardship and System Reform
articles.authors: Michael McGuinness
articles.abstract: Australia’s regulatory system is large, fragmented, and structurally opaque. No jurisdiction maintains a complete inventory of its regulators, and no national body tracks the full extent of regulatory authority across the federation. This preprint presents a cross‑jurisdictional census of regulatory authority in Australia using a seven‑layer architecture model. The method identifies and classifies regulators across statutory agencies, embedded units, national schemes, tribunals, and forms of regulatory dark matter. The findings indicate that Australia operates between 1,244 and 1,404 regulatory bodies and loci of authority, with a central estimate of approximately 1,360. Every jurisdiction, regardless of population size, operates a triple‑digit regulatory system. The study contributes a replicable census method, a seven‑layer model of regulatory architecture, and an account of how regulatory authority is distributed across jurisdictions and institutional layers. These findings have important implications for regulatory governance, visibility, coordination, and system stewardship.

articles.title: Exploring Administrative Burden and Burnout Among Italian Oncologists: A Cross‑Sectional Survey
articles.authors: Davide Petruzzelli; Monica Giordano; Giuseppe Aprile; Alexia Bertuzzi; Luisa Fioretto; Alessandro Marra; Paola Varese; Davide Integlia
articles.abstract: Administrative burden has become an increasingly prominent organizational issue in healthcare, with potential consequences for clinical practice, quality of care, and physician well‑being. This study assessed the extent and perceived impact of administrative burden among Italian medical oncologists through a national cross‑sectional web‑based survey conducted between February and March 2025 among clinicians affiliated with the Italian College of Chiefs of Medical Oncology Units (CIPOMO). A total of 179 valid questionnaires were analysed. Oncologists reported dedicating an average of 41.0% of their working time to administrative activities, predominantly related to form completion, management of information‑technology system failures, and data entry or data management. Activities most frequently perceived as appropriate for delegation included information‑technology troubleshooting, inventory control, patient reception, and appointment scheduling. Nearly half of respondents reported experiencing burnout in the previous six months, with over one fifth identifying administrative workload as the primary contributing factor. These findings indicate that administrative burden represents a substantial component of oncologists’ daily work and is perceived as limiting time available for core clinical activities, while contributing to professional distress. Organizational strategies aimed at redistributing non‑clinical tasks and strengthening administrative support may help mitigate these effects and promote more sustainable oncology practice.

articles.title: Dressed for the Festival, Made for the Landfill: Dark Patterns, Cultural Appropriation, and Greenwashing in Indian Fashion E-Commerce
articles.authors: Amrita
articles.abstract: India's fashion e-commerce sector is among the world's fastest-growing, with the online fashion retail segment projected to expand at a compound annual growth rate of 24.2% between 2025 and 2032. Major Indian fashion platforms routinely deploy culturally resonant visual design, including festival imagery, regional craft motifs, handloom aesthetics, and artisan-adjacent language, as central drivers of seasonal consumption. This paper develops a conceptual framework for understanding how such design choices perform a double function: they accelerate fast fashion purchasing by anchoring it in cultural legitimacy, while simultaneously appropriating the sustainability signifiers of the craft traditions they visually invoke. Drawing on scholarship in human-computer interaction, design ethics, consumer behavior, and postcolonial cultural studies, as well as first-hand practice-based research in Indian craft documentation and festive retail design, this paper makes three connected theoretical claims. Indian festival retail interface design functions as a culturally amplified consumption accelerant, combining established dark-pattern mechanisms with emotional cultural legitimacy in ways that existing HCI literature has not examined together. Handloom and craft aesthetics are regularly extracted from their social and ecological contexts and redeployed as sustainability signals in campaigns that primarily sell industrially produced fast fashion. Sustainability programs appended to volume-consumption architectures are structurally incapable of addressing the harm that the core interface is designed to produce. The paper closes by proposing a research agenda for empirical work and directions for responsible design practice at the intersection of cultural heritage, e-commerce UX, and environmental accountability.

articles.title: A sustainability conceptual classification system to support socio-economic indicator design and evaluation in fisheries and aquaculture
articles.authors: Amber Himes-Cornell; Kristin Hoelting; Lucy Bowser; Chiara Buonfrate; Gwendal Le Fol; Xavier Nelson-Rowntree; Priscilla Rivas
articles.abstract: Social and economic sustainability indicators are widely used to inform fisheries and aquaculture management, yet there is no common framework for developing, selecting, evaluating, or comparing them. A shared framework would bring coherence to this landscape by supporting the systematic identification of gaps and opportunities to strengthen understanding of social and economic dimensions across both sectors. In response to this need, the United Nations Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) and the ICES Working Group on Social Indicators undertook a scoping literature review to better understand how social and economic indicators have been used in fisheries and aquaculture management. This article describes the development and iterative refinement of a conceptual classification system, grounded in established sustainability frameworks and refined through pilot testing, that FAO developed to guide the selection and use of social and economic sustainability indicators in these two sectors. The system organizes the diverse concepts represented by indicators in the fisheries and aquaculture literature into a coherent set of categories and sub-categories. It offers a practical tool for evaluating and comparing the conceptual comprehensiveness of existing data collection efforts and for supporting the robust design of indicators in new initiatives.

articles.title: Crypto for the few? Social inequality and the embedding of cryptocurrency in wealth portfolios
articles.authors: Barbara Binder; Nora Müller
articles.abstract:
Cryptocurrencies have become an increasingly prominent component of household wealth portfolios. While existing research has primarily examined who owns cryptocurrencies, much less is known about how cryptocurrencies are embedded within broader portfolio structures and whether crypto investors constitute a distinct social group. Using an online survey of adults in Germany (N = 1,207), this study examines who invests in cryptocurrencies, how crypto is embedded within broader asset portfolios, and 
whether crypto investors form a homogenous or internally differentiated group. We combine logistic regression models with latent class and latent profile analyses. Logistic regressions identify socio-demographic, economic, attitudinal, and ideological correlates of cryptocurrency ownership. The latent class analysis examines how cryptocurrencies are embedded within broader ownership portfolios, while the latent profile analysis investigates heterogeneity in asset values among crypto-oriented investors. 
Cryptocurrency ownership is strongly stratified by age, gender, and risk tolerance, while associations with education and income become less pronounced once ideological orientations are considered. Portfolio analyses provide little evidence for a distinct crypto investor class. Instead, cryptocurrency ownership is embedded within broader structures of wealth accumulation and financial participation. Crypto investors are distributed across multiple portfolio profiles, including a financially oriented crypto-intensive group and a highly diversified asset elite that combines cryptocurrencies with conventional assets. Overall, the findings suggest that cryptocurrencies primarily extend rather than fundamentally transform existing patterns of financial inequality. By jointly analyzing ownership, portfolio composition, and asset values, the study contributes to sociological research on financialization, digital assets, and wealth inequality.

articles.title: Reflecting on Social Bias: Challenges and Opportunities for Computational Social Science
articles.authors: Ahrabhi Kathirgamalingam; Aytalina Kulichkina; Jana Bernhard-Harrer; Valerie Hase
articles.abstract: Computational social science (CSS) is an evolving field with transformative potential to expose and address pressing societal challenges. Yet, because CSS, much like other scientific disciplines, operates within structural contexts addressing social bias remains a critical issue requiring attention in the field. Social bias, defined as the systematic discrimination and stereotyping of groups and individuals, relates to CSS in multiple ways. Drawing on literature, we critically examine these connections across three perspectives: understanding, detecting, and mitigating social bias. First, we provide a foundation for understanding how social bias shapes both CSS as a research field and its methodology. Second, we highlight how social bias can be detected not only within the CSS methodology itself but also through CSS applied to broader societal domains. Finally, moving forward, we propose strategies to mitigate social bias in the CSS methodology and CSS as a research field. With our contribution, we seek to demonstrate the breadth of ways in which social bias and CSS intersect and to chart paths toward a more equitable and robust CSS.

articles.title: Access Is Not Action: An Agent-Based Model of Climate Information Flow and Usability in Institutional Networks
articles.authors: No authors listed
articles.abstract: This study examines the conditions under which climate information becomes usable within institutional networks. Although substantial efforts have been devoted to improving the production and dissemination of climate information, a persistent gap remains between information availability and its practical use in adaptation decision-making. Existing research has identified numerous barriers and enabling conditions affecting climate information use, but less attention has been given to how these factors interact within institutional networks to shape usability outcomes. To address this gap, we develop an empirically grounded agent-based model of climate information flow and usability based on the network of ICLEI Europe and its member municipalities. The model integrates three complementary empirical sources: survey data on information creation, request, sharing, and exchange; qualitative interviews examining how climate information is translated into institutional action; and secondary datasets used as proxy indicators for institutional, political, and contextual conditions. Together, these data inform both the structure of the network and the behavioural mechanisms governing information use. The model simulates the creation, exchange, and evaluation of climate information between municipalities and explores how institutional capacity, peer support, co-creation, and political barriers influence usability. Results show that information availability within the network is consistently high, with most information requests being fulfilled. However, information use remains comparatively low, revealing a persistent access–use gap. Sensitivity analyses indicate that usability is primarily shaped by accumulated experience, peer support, and political and institutional barriers. Strong peer networks and collaborative experience can substantially improve usability. Methodologically, the study demonstrates how survey data, interview findings, and proxy indicators can be systematically integrated into an agent-based modelling framework. Substantively, it highlights that improving adaptation outcomes requires greater attention to the conditions that enable information use, rather than focusing exclusively on increasing information provision.

articles.title: Picking Winners or Marking Them? Timing-Based Evaluation of Innovation Certification
articles.authors: No authors listed
articles.abstract:
Voluntary certification policies — which let firms self-nominate for innovative or privileged status — increasingly channel fiscal, financial, and regulatory support, yet evaluating them is deceptively hard: certified firms may outperform because certification works, or simply because firms apply when already rising. We show that voluntary certification can certify winners precisely because rational firms time entry to moments of transitory expansion: the measured premium largely reflects when firms participate, not what participation does.
We formalise this as anticipatory take-up: because the benefits are most valuable while a firm scales, forward-looking firms register at the crest of a growth run-up. The mechanism yields three predictions — a cross-sectional premium, a premium that largely predates certification, and entry driven by recent growth, not profitability.
We test them in the Italian innovative-SME regime, linking panel data for roughly 2,900 certified and 1,200 non-certified SMEs to each firm's registration date and sector, and treating performance as a six-dimensional vector. Certified firms grow far faster than balanced peers yet are financially less solid, especially when smaller and capital-intensive. Once registration timing is exploited through event-study and staggered difference-in-differences designs, the premium proves largely selection: about 81 per cent of the revenue advantage predates registration, and the residual merely continues a pre-existing trend. A hazard model confirms it — entry rises with recent growth and smaller size, not profitability.
The paper reframes the evaluation of voluntary certification from average effects to observable dynamic selection: such schemes mark firms already on distinctive trajectories rather than create them. The lesson generalises — robustness to omitted variables is not robustness to selection on trends, and cross-sectional evaluations mislead unless they exploit the timing of take-up.

articles.title: Social Heterogeneity and Technology Adoption in Colonial India
articles.authors: No authors listed
articles.abstract: Our paper examines whether local social heterogeneity influenced the adoption of ring-spinning technology in colonial Indian cotton mills during the interwar period. While existing literature emphasises factor prices, raw material constraints, and managerial choices, we highlight the role of social structure in shaping firm level technological outcomes. Using a mill level dataset coupled with census-based caste heterogeneity and religious composition, we analyse variation in the scale and intensity of ring-spinning adoption. We find that caste heterogeneity was negatively associated with the absolute numbers of ring spindle adoption. In contrast, religious composition does not significantly affect the scale of adoption but is positively associated with ring intensity which is share of ring in total spindles, indicating a distinct channel through which social structure shaped mill level technological configuration. These findings contribute to the economic history of technological diffusion by demonstrating that social heterogeneity influenced firms’ ability to organise labour and scale modern technologies, highlighting the importance of social structure for industrial development.
